Centralizes the display of user-friendly error or empty states when id is missing, getProductDetails fails, or no product data is returned. When an error is triggered by url-state-manager or product-detail-loader, this component shows an appropriate message and hides purchase and recommendation components to prevent invalid actions.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a stacked, full-width structure similar to the mockup: a slim announcement/status bar at the very top, followed by the global navigation (assumed from the site shell), then the main product hero section filling most of the first viewport, and below that a recommendations band. The product hero mimics the mockup’s two-column featured section: a bold, colored left column for the book’s visual identity and a clean right column for title, details, and purchase actions. All interior content sits inside a centered max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a stacked, full-width structure similar to the mockup: a slim announcement/status bar at the very top, followed by the global navigation (assumed from the site shell), then the main product hero section filling most of the first viewport, and below that a recommendations band. The product hero mimics the mockup’s two-column featured section: a bold, colored left column for the book’s visual identity and a clean right column for title, details, and purchase actions. All interior content sits inside a centered max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 10–15% of the viewport is consumed by the header/status+nav area. The primary product hero (book details and purchase area) uses roughly 55–65% of the first viewport height, so the user immediately sees the ebook, price, and key actions without feeling crowded. The recommendations section sits below with generous vertical margins (32–48px between sections, 48–64px between hero and recommendations). Horizontally, the product hero’s two columns follow a 40% (visual/left) / 60% (text & actions/right) split inside the 1100–1200px centered container.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains moderately spacious: header/status content is compact but not cramped, the product hero has comfortable breathing room around the cover, title, and buttons, and the recommendations grid shows a curated number of items per row (e.g., 3–4) with clear card spacing. Padding and margins follow the 8px base system, with 16–24px padding inside major blocks and 32–48px vertical separation between main sections. Elements needed for quick commerce (price, primary CTA) are prominent but not overcrowded; secondary information (specs, sample link, wishlist) is visible but slightly de-emphasized.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the dark header/status bar at the very top, then moves into the center logo and global nav (outside this component list), and naturally drops into the main product hero. Within the hero, the first strong pull is the left-side colored book panel (cover and key highlights); from there, the gaze crosses to the right into the large title, price, and purchase actions, following a left-to-right reading pattern. Below, the flow continues downward to the recommendations grid. Feedback elements (confirmation messages, error states) appear near the top center/right, intercepting the user’s attention immediately after actions without obscuring the main product layout.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page appears as a modern, image-forward ebook storefront framed by a dark header. At the very top, a slim, full-width black status bar announces key information across the entire screen. Beneath this, the site’s global navigation sits in a charcoal band, with content centered and spaced according to the 12-column grid. The main product area begins just below the header and behaves like an editorial hero: inside a centered, max-width container, the book is presented in a two-column layout. The left column occupies roughly two-fifths of the width and uses a rich red background panel to showcase the ebook cover and select highlights, echoing the bold red feature block from the mockup. The right column, on a white or very light background, occupies the remaining width and presents the book’s title in large type at the top, followed by author, price, primary purchase button, wishlist toggle, and a link to sample content, all arranged vertically with generous spacing. The specifications block sits either below these actions in the same right column or immediately underneath the two-column hero as a full-width, lightly bordered section, giving breathing room to detailed metadata. As the user scrolls past the hero, a well-spaced section introduces recommended ebooks in a grid, spanning the same centered content width and leveraging large book covers and clean typography. System-level components—loaders, URL state management, cart and wishlist summaries—operate behind the scenes or occupy subtle positions near the header, while confirmation toasts and error states appear near the top edge of the viewport, momentarily dominating attention without breaking the overall calm, editorial flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and sits at the very top of the page hierarchy, conceptually above the header/status bar. It initializes the page state before any visual elements render, so it does not occupy any measurable width or height in the visual layout and does not alter spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; runs before and alongside layout rendering, not participating in the DOM’s visible flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-detail-loader controls the loading state for the main product hero area. Visually, when loading, it occupies the same region as the eventual product hero: a centered block below the header, spanning the full content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and about 55–65% of the viewport height. While active, it may show a skeleton or shimmer that respects the two-column structure—placeholder blocks on the left for the cover and on the right for title and actions—without adding extra vertical space beyond the main hero’s footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the main content flow; it temporarily replaces the product-main-info and associated components until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-loader is visually subtle and aligned with the header/cart icon area rather than the main body. It supports the cart status displayed in the global header’s right segment. While loading, it may show a small spinner or subtle placeholder near the cart icon but does not introduce new rows or bands; it fits within the header’s existing vertical height and rightmost 30% segment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the header’s right utility area; non-blocking and does not affect the main content’s vertical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Similar to the cart-summary-loader, this loader supports wishlist indicators that may appear in the header or near the wishlist toggle in the hero. Any visual indicator remains compact—no more than a small icon-level spinner or subtle text—sitting adjacent to the wishlist icon in the header or next to the wishlist toggle beside the main CTA. It does not alter the overall header height or hero column widths.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-disruptive, occupying only the local inline space around the wishlist icon or toggle.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-main-info component forms the core of the left hero column and the top of the right column. Inside the main content container, just below the header, the page divides into two columns: the left column occupying about 40% of the container width and the right column about 60%, both extending down roughly 55–65% of the viewport height. Product-main-info’s visual emphasis appears in the left column: a tall, solid red background panel touches the top of the hero section and extends downward with 24–32px of interior padding. Centered within this red panel is a large ebook cover image (approximately 60–70% of the panel’s width), with the book title or short promotional copy above or below in white or light text. A slight drop shadow or subtle border-radius can visually lift the cover off the red background. In the right column, product-main-info contributes the main title and author line at the very top of the column: the title spans the full right column width, using large, minimalist typography, followed by author and key metadata aligned left with 16–24px vertical spacing between lines. This creates a strong left-to-right visual transition from the red cover block into the large title text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main hero section; its left and right portions respect the two-column grid and shrink vertically on smaller screens, collapsing to a stacked layout (cover panel on top, text below) when the viewport is narrow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specification-block appears as a clean, textual band for detailed information. In the default desktop view, it sits in the right hero column directly below the primary title, author, and purchase area. It spans the full width of the right column (about 60% of the container) and is separated from the purchase-actions by 24–32px of vertical space. Inside, specifications are arranged in a tidy list or two-column detail grid with 16px vertical spacing between rows, maintaining a lightweight, editorial feel. On longer pages or in cases with extensive specs, the block may extend beyond the visual height of the left column but remains visually anchored to the right. On narrower screens, it shifts to full-width below the stacked hero content, with 32–40px top margin to preserve hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, flowing vertically within the main content; expands downward as needed without changing the width allocations of the hero columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchase-actions component sits prominently in the upper half of the right hero column, immediately beneath the title and key metadata provided by product-main-info. It occupies roughly the left two-thirds of the right column’s width, aligned flush left with the title, leaving some whitespace to the right to maintain a light, editorial feel. The primary CTA button (e.g., “Buy ebook”) is large and horizontally oriented, ~48px tall with 16–24px horizontal padding, and appears about 16–24px below the price. Secondary options (e.g., preorder, different formats) stack vertically beneath, each separated by 8–16px. The entire purchase-actions block takes up about one-quarter to one-third of the right column’s height, ensuring that on a typical laptop view, the user sees title, price, and primary CTA without scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right hero column; maintains its vertical position near the top of the column even as specifications or other details expand below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-toggle is visually linked to the purchase-actions in the right hero column. It appears either to the immediate right of the primary CTA button (on wide screens) or directly below it (on tighter layouts), separated by 8–16px of horizontal or vertical space. On desktop, if placed to the right, it occupies roughly one-third of the width of the purchase-actions row, aligning vertically with the center of the main button to create a balanced, compact control area. The toggle uses a minimalist icon-plus-label style, with subdued colors compared to the primary CTA, ensuring it reads as secondary but still easily discoverable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the purchase-actions component; it reflows from side-by-side to stacked below the primary button as horizontal space decreases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sample-content-link is placed just below the purchase-actions (and wishlist-toggle) within the right hero column. It is left-aligned with the title and CTAs, appearing as a text link or subtle ghost button with a small icon. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the purchase-actions block and this link, giving it room to breathe while clearly associating it with the act of exploring the book before buying. The link itself is compact, occupying only the natural width of its label (e.g., about 20–30% of the right column width), preserving ample whitespace on the right side of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, stacked within the right hero column’s vertical flow; moves down below the wishlist-toggle when the layout collapses on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results-link appears at the very top of the main content area, just below the global navigation/header and above the two-column product hero. It is left-aligned within the main content container, starting roughly 24–32px below the header’s bottom edge. The link is small and understated—an icon plus label or simple text—occupying only its natural width (around 10–15% of the container’s width), with ample whitespace around it. Below this link, a larger 24–32px vertical gap leads into the product hero, reinforcing that the link is a navigational affordance rather than part of the hero content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the top of the content flow; remains fixed relative to the hero, staying visible only when the user is near the top of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommendations-loader controls loading for the recommendations section that appears below the product hero. It occupies a full-width band inside the main content container starting approximately 48–64px beneath the bottom of the hero section. While loading, it may display a row of 3–4 skeleton cards aligned to the same grid as the eventual recommendations or a centered spinner with a short label. This band spans the content width (about 1100–1200px) but does not exceed a moderate vertical height—roughly 200–300px—so it feels like a natural continuation of the page rather than a separate full-screen overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline below the hero section; it is replaced by the recommendations-grid once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommendations-grid forms a dedicated section beneath the main product hero and specifications. It begins roughly 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge, within the same centered container. A section heading (e.g., “You may also like”) appears at the top of this band, left-aligned and spanning nearly the full content width, with 16–24px of space below it. The grid itself displays 3–4 product cards per row on desktop, each card occupying an equal fraction of the width with 16–24px gutters between them. Cards are vertically spacious, featuring prominent cover images and concise titles, matching the curated, editorial mood. The overall recommendations section height depends on the number of rows but generally occupies about 30–50% of the viewport height. Additional vertical margin (32–48px) below the last row separates this section from any footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, responsive grid; cards wrap into fewer columns on smaller viewports while maintaining consistent horizontal and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation-messages component appears as a small, unobtrusive notification center near the top of the viewport. When a user adds an item to cart or wishlist, a message slides or fades in from the top-right corner, just below the header band, occupying roughly 25–35% of the viewport width on desktop with a maximum width of about 360–400px. The toast is aligned to the right edge of the content container, not the full browser window, to maintain alignment with the main layout. It floats above the content with a subtle shadow, separated from the header by 8–16px of vertical space, and auto-dismisses or can be manually closed. Multiple messages stack vertically in this same region with 8–12px gaps between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating, positioned fixed or sticky relative to the viewport’s top-right area; overlays the main content without shifting layout, always anchored just beneath the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-status-bar is the slim announcement strip at the very top of the page, echoing the black bar from the mockup. It spans the full browser width and sits above the main navigation. Its height is modest—about 32–40px—with content (such as an all-caps message about offers or account status) centered horizontally within the same max-width content band used for the rest of the layout. Text is centered or slightly left-biased depending on content, and there is 8–12px of vertical padding inside. This bar visually anchors the top of the page and establishes the dark chrome framing around the rich content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport or scrolling with the page depending on global header behavior; it always appears above the navigation and does not overlap main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-empty-state-handler manages states where the product data cannot be loaded or is missing. In error or empty states, it replaces the main product hero and recommendations area with a centered message. This message appears within the main content container, vertically centered in the remaining viewport height below the header, occupying about 40–60% of the viewport height and up to 60% of the content width. The text is center-aligned with a concise headline and supporting copy, surrounded by generous whitespace (at least 32px padding on all sides). Optional actions (e.g., “Back to catalog”) appear as buttons beneath the message, centered horizontally. The header/status bar and global navigation remain visible above, preserving layout context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the main body content; when active, it suppresses the display of product-main-info, purchase-actions, recommendations, and related loaders within the main content area.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Page to review and manage ebooks in the shopping cart, apply promo codes, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load the active cart with full line-item details (including associated products and applied promo codes) using getCartDetails and keep it updated after any cart mutation.
Display each cart item (ebook or preorder) with product information, quantity, price, and line subtotal, and allow quantity changes via updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow removal of individual cart items via removeCartItem and full cart clearing via clearCart, with cart totals recalculated after each action.
Show an order summary section bound to Cart.subtotal, Cart.discountTotal, and Cart.total, with additional preorder indication derived from CartItem.isPreorder or getCartSummary.hasPreorders.
Provide a promo code input form that calls applyPromoCode to add valid codes (e.g., READ10) and update cart totals, and allow removal of applied promo codes via removePromoCode.
Display any currently active promotions using getActivePromotions so users can see available promo codes relevant to the cart.

Expose navigation actions: proceed to checkout (navigate to checkout.html), continue shopping (navigate to browse.html?categoryId=all_ebooks), and view product details (navigate to product.html?id={productId}&returnUrl=cart.html) from each cart item.
Keep header-level cart and wishlist indicators in sync using getCartSummary (for item count and totals) and getWishlistSummary (for wishlist item count).
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Review cart on page load: 1) User navigates to cart.html (optionally with URL params). 2) Page initialization component calls getCartDetails to retrieve cart, items, and appliedPromos. 3) In parallel, it calls getCartSummary, getWishlistSummary, and getActivePromotions for header and promotional info. 4) If a promoCode URL parameter is present and no matching promo is already applied, the page calls applyPromoCode with that code and refreshes cart state from the response. 5) All components (item list, summary, promo form, promotions banner, header badges) bind to the loaded data.

Workflow 2 – Change item quantity or remove an item: 1) User edits quantity control on a cart line item. 2) Component calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cartItemId and new quantity. 3) On success, the component updates local cart state using the returned Cart and optionally triggers a refresh via getCartDetails for item-level details; getCartSummary may be called to update header summary. 4) If the user chooses to remove an item instead, the item component calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId, then refreshes cart state similarly.

Workflow 3 – Apply or remove a promo code: 1) User types a promo code into the promo form and submits. 2) Component calls applyPromoCode with the entered code. 3) If success is true, the component updates its local Cart and applied promo list from the response and reflects updated subtotal, discountTotal, and total in the summary panel (and may refresh getCartSummary for header consistency). 4) If success is false, the component shows message to the user without changing cart data. 5) When the user clicks to remove a specific promo, the component calls removePromoCode with that code, then updates Cart and totals from the response.

Workflow 4 – Clear cart and continue shopping: 1) User clicks a 'Clear cart' action. 2) Component calls clearCart. 3) On success, the component replaces the current cart state with the returned empty Cart and updates dependent components (items list becomes empty state, summary shows zero totals, promo list clears). 4) Optionally, user clicks 'Continue shopping', which navigates to browse.html?categoryId=all_ebooks using standard URL navigation.

Workflow 5 – Proceed to checkout or view product details: 1) After reviewing items, totals, and promos, user clicks 'Proceed to checkout'. 2) The summary component checks that the current Cart.total is greater than zero (based on bound data) and then navigates to checkout.html (no additional parameters). 3) If the user wants to inspect an ebook before checkout, they click the item’s title or cover, and the item list component navigates to product.html?id={productId}&returnUrl=cart.html so that the product page can return the user to the cart.
interactions: On page load, the cart-page loader automatically calls getCartDetails, getCartSummary, getWishlistSummary, and getActivePromotions, then distributes the resulting data to dependent components.
When the URL contains a promoCode query parameter, the loader attempts to apply that promo by calling applyPromoCode with the code and merges the returned Cart state into the current view.
Clicking a product title or cover in the cart item list triggers navigation to product.html with the product’s id and a returnUrl=cart.html parameter.

Changing a quantity input or stepper on a cart item triggers updateCartItemQuantity with that item’s cartItemId and the new quantity value; on success, the cart item list and summary panel are refreshed from the updated Cart.
Clicking a remove/ delete control on a cart item triggers removeCartItem with the item’s cartItemId and, on success, removes it from the list and updates totals.
Submitting the promo code form triggers applyPromoCode with the current input value; the promo form displays the response message and updates the displayed applied promo list and cart totals.
Clicking a remove control next to an applied promo triggers removePromoCode with that promo’s code and updates cart totals and applied promos based on the response.
Clicking the 'Clear cart' action triggers clearCart and, upon completion, causes the cart item list to render an empty state and the summary panel to show zeroed amounts.
Clicking the 'Continue shopping' action navigates the user to browse.html?categoryId=all_ebooks via standard link navigation.

Clicking the 'Proceed to checkout' button in the summary panel navigates to checkout.html, preserving any relevant query parameters from the current URL only if necessary according to global navigation rules (the Cart state itself remains server-side).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Cart page is primarily identified by the URL cart.html without required query parameters; the active cart is managed server-side and retrieved via getCartDetails and getCartSummary. Optional URL parameters are used to influence initial state and navigation behavior: (1) promoCode={code}: if present on load and the corresponding promo is not already applied to the cart, the page calls applyPromoCode with this code and replaces its cart state with the response. Any result messages are shown in the promo form component. (2) messageType and messageKey (optional): these generic parameters can be inspected by the loader to display non-persistent system messages (e.g., post-redirect success or error) without relying on localStorage; they are interpreted only for display and do not alter cart data. (3) Standard navigation parameters from other pages (for example, product.html might send returnUrl=cart.html when linking back) do not affect cart logic but may be preserved when navigating away if consistent with global routing rules. UI state such as expanded/collapsed sections or currently focused form fields is maintained in client memory only and is not encoded in the URL. All cart mutations (item quantity changes, removals, promo code changes, clearing the cart) are performed exclusively through the assigned interfaces, and after each mutation the cart state in memory is replaced or updated using the Cart object returned by the interface, ensuring consistency without using localStorage.

components.functionality: Initializes and orchestrates data loading for the cart page, retrieving cart details, summary, wishlist summary, and active promotions; interprets URL parameters (such as promoCode) to optionally auto-apply a promo on load; propagates updated Cart and related data to child components after any cart mutation.

Renders the list of cart items using the items array from getCartDetails, including ebook title, author (from Product), format, preorder indication (from CartItem.isPreorder and Product.availabilityStatus), unit price, quantity control, line total, and remove action; delegates navigation to product details and triggers cart update operations on quantity change or item removal.

Displays aggregated monetary information for the current cart, including subtotal, discountTotal, total, and any preorder warning or info messages derived from CartItem.isPreorder or getCartSummary.hasPreorders; enables the 'Proceed to checkout' action and can block or warn if the total is zero or there are no items.

Provides an input field for entering a promo code and an apply action that calls applyPromoCode; lists applied promo codes (from getCartDetails.appliedPromos or Cart.promoCodeIds resolved via the initial data set) with the ability to remove each via removePromoCode; shows feedback messages based on the success and message values returned from promo-related calls.

Fetches and displays a list of currently active promotions by calling getActivePromotions; exposes the promo code values and descriptions so users can manually enter them into the promo-code-form without hardcoding any specific code on the page.

Provides global cart actions such as clearing the cart and continuing shopping; when the user clears the cart, it calls clearCart and notifies the cart-page-loader to refresh all dependent components; the continue shopping control navigates to browse.html with categoryId=all_ebooks.

Displays a functional empty-cart message and a 'Continue shopping' link when getCartDetails returns a Cart with no items; this component is mutually exclusive with the cart-item-list and summary-panel when the cart is empty.

Represents the cart icon/indicator in the site header as seen on the cart page; consumes getCartSummary to show current itemCount and total or subtotal as appropriate; updates in response to cart mutations triggered on this page.

Represents the wishlist icon/indicator in the header; uses getWishlistSummary to show current wishlist item count and remains synchronized with wishlist operations that may occur elsewhere in the session.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page keeps the same linear, stacked structure as the mockup: announcement bar at the very top, then the global navigation/header (with cart and wishlist summaries in the right section), followed by the main cart content. Within the cart content, the primary pattern is a large, two-column band: cart items and related forms on the left, and a compact summary/checkout panel on the right, echoing the mockup’s featured-product two-column layout. Above the items, a slim promotions banner spans the content width; below them, a horizontal actions bar anchors the end of the flow. When the cart is empty, this two-column structure is replaced by a centered empty state block.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page keeps the same linear, stacked structure as the mockup: announcement bar at the very top, then the global navigation/header (with cart and wishlist summaries in the right section), followed by the main cart content. Within the cart content, the primary pattern is a large, two-column band: cart items and related forms on the left, and a compact summary/checkout panel on the right, echoing the mockup’s featured-product two-column layout. Above the items, a slim promotions banner spans the content width; below them, a horizontal actions bar anchors the end of the flow. When the cart is empty, this two-column structure is replaced by a centered empty state block.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (announcement + nav) occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport, mirroring the mockup. The main cart content sits in a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), with 48–64px of vertical space between the header and the first cart element, supporting the same editorial spaciousness. Inside the container on desktop, the left column (cart items + promo form) takes about 60–65% of the content width, while the right column (summary panel) takes about 35–40%, similar to the featured-book layout proportions but inverted in emphasis. Vertical gaps between sub-sections (banner, list, promo form, actions bar) are mostly 24–48px, with the largest separation before the actions bar and before the footer. On mobile, all components stack vertically to span nearly 100% of the container width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and curated. The cart list shows a limited number of items per scroll with generous 16–24px spacing between item cards and 16–24px padding inside each card, avoiding clutter. The summary panel is tightly organized but uses ample padding, white space, and clear grouping to keep totals legible. The promotions banner and actions bar are visually light strips with succinct text and minimal controls. Overall the page prioritizes clarity and breathing room over fitting in as many items or controls as possible, consistent with the mockup’s editorial, storefront feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the dark announcement and navigation bars, then immediately picks up the cart indicator in the top-right utilities area. From there, the eye drops into the main cart container where a full-width promotions banner provides the first focal band. The gaze then moves down to the left-column cart items (primary content), then across to the right-column summary panel (totals and checkout). As the user scrolls down the list, the right summary panel remains visually present (and can remain sticky on larger screens), reinforcing the path toward checkout. Finally, the eye is led to the bottom actions bar, which provides a strong final call-to-action, mirroring the mockup’s Z-like movement from top bar to right utilities, then down-left into primary content and across to the right-hand emphasis block.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark announcement bar and the charcoal navigation header, the cart page opens into a spacious, centered content band capped by a slim, full-width promotions banner with a subtle red-tinted background. The main body of the page is a two-column layout inside a max-width container: on the left, a vertical stack of clean, white cart item cards with generous spacing and simple typography; on the right, a compact, visually solid summary panel that echoes the mockup’s bold color blocks and houses the order total and checkout button. The left column continues downward into a neatly framed promo code form, while the right column’s summary panel stays visually anchored near the top (and can stick on scroll on desktop) to keep the payment summary in view. At the bottom of the content stack, a low-profile actions bar spans the container, aligning with both columns and offering secondary actions like continuing shopping alongside a reiterated checkout path. If the cart is empty, the two-column structure gives way to a centered, airy empty state block that sits just below the header, with an illustration or icon and a primary button, maintaining the same modern, image-forward and editorial feel seen in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page loader appears as a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers the entire viewport area below the global header, dimming the main cart content while data is loading. It stretches from the left edge to the right edge of the browser window and from just under the navigation bar down to the bottom of the viewport, following the full-bleed hero behavior from the mockup but in a neutral, functional style. At its center (both horizontally and vertically), a minimalist spinner or loading indicator is placed, sized to about 48–64px, with enough empty space (at least 64px of clear area) around it so it feels calm and unobtrusive. The header with the cart and wishlist indicators remains visible above the overlay, reinforcing the site structure while signaling that the cart content itself is in a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay that sits above all main content but below the global header, locking scroll while active and maintaining center alignment for the spinner across all viewport sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list occupies the left column of the main cart layout within the centered max-width container. On desktop and larger tablets, it begins approximately 48px below the bottom of the promotions banner, aligned flush with the left content margin, and spans about 60–65% of the container width. Each cart item is presented as a horizontally oriented card with a white or very light background, subtle shadow or border, and 16–24px of internal padding, stacked vertically with 16–24px of space between cards. The book cover thumbnail appears on the left side of each card, taking roughly 20–25% of the card width, while the title, author, price, and controls (quantity, remove, etc.) are arranged on the right side in a clean, typographic block, echoing the mockup’s balance between imagery and text. The list extends downward as needed, with at least 32–40px of space at the bottom before transitioning into the promo-code-form. On smaller screens, this list becomes a full-width column, centered within the container, with each card spanning nearly 100% of the width and preserving the vertical spacing for a spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page. On wide screens it consistently anchors the left side of the two-column grid; on narrow viewports it reflows to a single-column layout, always appearing above the promo-code-form and actions bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel sits in the right column of the main cart container, visually mirroring the bold color block from the mockup’s featured product section. On desktop, it aligns with the top of the cart-item-list, starting approximately 48px below the promotions banner and flush with the right content margin, spanning about 35–40% of the container width. The panel itself is a vertically oriented card with a strong, contrasting background (e.g., deep charcoal or muted warm tone) and generous padding (24–32px on all sides), creating a compact but prominent block. Inside, the order summary is stacked from top to bottom: heading, line items (subtotal, taxes, etc.), then a large primary checkout button that nearly spans the panel’s width, with 16–24px of spacing between each group. There is at least 24px of margin between the panel and the container’s left column, reinforcing clear separation from the item list. On taller pages where the item list extends further, the panel remains visually present in the upper right, and on large screens it can be sticky so that as the user scrolls the list, the summary remains pinned near the top-right quadrant of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the panel behaves as a sticky element relative to the main content area: it stays fixed within the right column with a small offset (e.g., 16–24px) from the top of the viewport while the user scrolls through cart items. On smaller screens, it loses stickiness and shifts below the cart-item-list, spanning full width with the same internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code-form is positioned directly beneath the cart-item-list within the left column of the main container, maintaining the linear flow. It begins about 32–40px below the last cart item card to create a clear separation between item management and discounts. The form itself spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the container on desktop) and appears as a low-profile, white or light-background bar with a subtle border or top divider. Inside, the promo input field sits on the left, occupying roughly 65–70% of the form width, while the ‘Apply’ button is on the right, taking the remaining 30–35%, both sharing a common baseline and aligned horizontally. Internal padding is 16–24px, with 16px vertical space above any helper text. On smaller viewports, the input and button stack vertically with a 8–16px gap, still keeping at least 24px of margin above the subsequent cart-actions-bar to maintain the mockup’s airy, editorial spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, flowing directly after the cart-item-list. On narrow screens, it reflows to a full-width block beneath the list, maintaining its relative order before the actions bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-promotions-banner sits at the very top of the main cart content area, immediately below the global header and page title region, and just above the two-column layout that contains the cart items and summary. It spans the full width of the max-width container, bridging both the left and right columns so it reads as one continuous horizontal band. The banner has a subtle red-tinted or warm-colored background that echoes the bold red rectangle from the mockup, but in a slimmer, less dominant strip roughly 56–72px tall. Text is centered vertically within the band and aligned left on desktop, with a brief, all-caps or uppercase headline and optional supporting line, leaving generous horizontal margins on both sides (24–32px). On small screens, the text may center-align but still maintains at least 16–24px of padding above and below. There is about 32–48px of space between the bottom of this banner and the top of both the cart-item-list (left) and the cart-summary-panel (right), reinforcing a clear section break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored directly under the header within the main content stack, always appearing above both the left and right columns. It spans full container width at all breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-actions-bar appears as the closing band of the cart content, sitting beneath the promo-code-form and vertically aligned with the full width of the main container so it visually ties together both columns. On desktop, it begins about 32–48px below the promo-code-form and stretches from the left to the right content margin. The bar has a subtle background (e.g., very light gray) or a top border to differentiate it from the white page background while staying understated. Inside, content is laid out horizontally: a ‘Continue Shopping’ link or secondary button is left-aligned, while primary action(s) such as ‘Proceed to Checkout’ or ‘Update Cart’ are right-aligned, mirroring the header’s left-right balance. There is 16–24px of vertical padding, and controls are spaced with 16px gaps to maintain clarity. On mobile, the bar becomes more prominent for usability: it spans full width at the bottom of the scrollable area and can optionally stick to the bottom of the viewport, with the primary checkout button centered or occupying nearly full width and secondary actions grouped beneath or above it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content on desktop and large tablets, creating a natural endpoint before the footer. On small screens, it can behave as a sticky bottom bar, remaining visible as the user scrolls through the cart to ensure easy access to checkout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state replaces the entire two-column layout when there are no items in the cart. In this mode, the main container becomes a single-centered block that appears just below the header and any announcement bar, with approximately 64–80px of top margin to create an airy, hero-like feel. The empty state content is centered horizontally within the container, spanning about 50–60% of the container width on desktop to maintain focus, and can include a small illustration or icon at the top, a bold, simple headline beneath it, a short explanatory line, and a primary button leading back to the catalog or homepage. Vertical spacing between these elements is generous (16–24px), with at least 48px of clear space below the primary button before the footer. Background remains mostly white or lightly tinted, with no right-column summary panel displayed, maintaining the clean, editorial look of a feature spread. On narrow devices, the empty state shrinks to about 80–90% of the viewport width while remaining center-aligned and vertically offset enough from the header to feel spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the central portion of the viewport. It is mutually exclusive with the cart-item-list, promo-code-form, summary panel, and actions bar; when active, those components are hidden and the empty state forms the core of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-summary is placed in the rightmost segment of the navigation/header bar, aligned horizontally with the search control and the wishlist indicator. It sits near the right edge of the max-width header content band, with 16–24px of padding from the edge, and appears as a cart icon accompanied by a small item count badge or label. In the overall 30/40/30 header distribution, it occupies a small but visually important part of the rightmost 30%, aligned on the same vertical centerline as the logo and nav links. The badge uses a red or accent-colored circle or pill reminiscent of the mockup’s red focal block, making the cart state immediately visible against the dark header background. There is a modest 16px horizontal gap between the wishlist summary and this cart summary to avoid crowding while still reading as a cohesive utility cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header, which itself can be sticky at the top of the viewport. The cart summary remains visible at all times as the user scrolls, serving as a persistent entry point to the cart page and reflecting live item counts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-wishlist-summary sits directly to the left of the header-cart-summary within the right-hand utilities section of the navigation bar. It shares the same vertical alignment as the cart summary and search control, and is positioned so that the eye naturally moves from the center logo to the right search icon and then to the wishlist and cart icons. The wishlist indicator uses a more subtle visual treatment than the cart—such as a heart icon with a smaller, less saturated count badge or simple numeric label—so it reads as secondary but still accessible. The icon-plus-count group takes up minimal width, leaving sufficient spacing (around 16px) on both sides to harmonize with the overall open, uncluttered header. Against the dark header background, it is legible but less attention-grabbing than the cart summary, preserving the cart as the primary focal point on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sticky header, always visible at the top of the viewport. It sits immediately to the left of the header-cart-summary and to the right of the search control, maintaining a consistent right-hand utilities cluster across all pages.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Review your ebook order, enter purchaser and payment details, and submit payment to place purchases and preorders on a single checkout page.

core_features: Load and display a concise checkout summary using getCheckoutSummary, including all cart items (purchases and preorders), promotions, and pricing totals.
Display a compact cart and wishlist status in the header using getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to keep users aware of their current item counts and totals.
Collect purchaser details (name and email) in a dedicated form section without requiring user account creation; bind these values for use when placing the order.
Collect payment method selection and method-specific payment details, structure them into a paymentDetails payload, and validate required fields before allowing order submission.

Present preorder-specific information when the current cart contains preorder items (detected via getCheckoutSummary items or getCartSummary.hasPreorders), including release-date and delivery behavior notes.
Require explicit agreement to terms and conditions (and any preorder policy) via a checkbox before enabling the Place order action; pass this consent to placeOrder via agreeToTerms.

Submit the order via placeOrder using purchaserName, purchaserEmail, paymentMethod, paymentDetails, and agreeToTerms, and handle success or failure states in-page without redirecting to another HTML file.

Render an inline order confirmation state when placeOrder succeeds, including order metadata, ordered items, and any preorder indicators (using the returned Order and OrderItem data), along with a confirmation message from the API response.
Show inline error messages and keep form fields populated if placeOrder fails, allowing users to correct issues (such as missing required fields or payment errors) and resubmit.
Provide a Back to cart navigation control that returns the user to cart.html while preserving any existing query parameters that do not conflict with the checkout page.
user_workflows: 1) Arrive at checkout: The user clicks a Proceed to checkout control on cart.html and is navigated to checkout.html, optionally with a step URL parameter. On page load, the page initializes header context (calling getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary) and core order data (calling getCheckoutSummary). If getCheckoutSummary returns an empty cart or an error, the page guides the user back to cart.html.

2) Review order items and totals: The user reviews a list of items from getCheckoutSummary.items with product and cartItem details, including preorder status where applicable. The user sees subtotal, discountTotal, total, and appliedPromos. If the user decides to change items, they click Back to cart to navigate to cart.html.

3) Enter purchaser details: In the Purchaser details component, the user enters their full name and email address required for receipt and ebook delivery. As they type, the component updates local form state bound to Order.purchaserName and Order.purchaserEmail. Field-level validation runs on blur or form submission (for example, checking that email is non-empty and properly formatted).

4) Choose payment method and enter payment details: The user selects a paymentMethod (card, paypal, or other) in the payment section. Based on the selected method, the component displays the appropriate input set (for example, card details for card, an intent to redirect for paypal, or a generic reference field for other). The data entered is collected into a structured paymentDetails object to be passed unchanged to placeOrder.

5) Acknowledge terms and preorder policy: If getCartSummary.hasPreorders is true or any cart item has isPreorder true, the page highlights preorder messaging explaining that preorder items will be delivered according to their releaseDate and may appear differently in the confirmation. The user must tick an Agree to terms checkbox which sets agreeToTerms to true in local state.

6) Place the order: When all required form sections pass validation and agreeToTerms is true, the Place order button becomes enabled. The user submits the order, which triggers a single call to placeOrder with purchaserName, purchaserEmail, paymentMethod, paymentDetails, and agreeToTerms from the current form state. A loading state disables form interactions until the response returns.

7) Handle successful order placement: If placeOrder returns success true, the page stops showing inline validation errors, updates any header summaries by re-calling getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary (to reflect the emptied or checked-out cart), reads the returned Order and items arrays, and switches the main content to a confirmation view. The confirmation view shows a structured summary (for example, a confirmation message, orderNumber, totals, purchaserEmail, and a list of items with preorder flags via OrderItem.isPreorder and Order.includesPreorders). The URL step parameter is updated to reflect that the page is in confirmation mode (for example, step=confirmation) so that if the user navigates backward within the same session, the browser history reflects the state transition.

8) Handle failed order placement: If placeOrder returns success false, the page displays the returned message near the top of the checkout form and may highlight the relevant sections (purchaser details, payment, or terms) depending on any error codes extracted from the message. The form fields remain populated with the user input to allow corrections. The URL step parameter is left unchanged (for example, remaining in step=payment) so that the user understands they are still in the payment stage.

9) Navigate away or adjust order: At any point before placing the order, the user can click the Back to cart control, which navigates to cart.html with any non-checkout-specific query parameters preserved. If the user revisits checkout.html after modifying the cart, a fresh getCheckoutSummary call runs and the form resets to its initial state (step=review, empty purchaser and payment fields).
interactions: On page load, the page triggers initialization logic: it reads the URL query parameters (such as step and any error codes passed from previous navigation), then calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to populate header components, and calls getCheckoutSummary to populate the main order summary panel. If any of these calls fail, an error state is displayed in their respective components.

Clicking the Back to cart link or button triggers a navigation event to cart.html. The component may carry over non-checkout-specific query parameters while removing checkout-specific ones such as step to avoid conflicting states on the cart page.

Inside the order summary panel, users can expand or collapse additional per-item information if the component provides it (for example, preorder notes or product metadata); these are purely client-side interactions and do not trigger additional API calls.

Typing into the purchaser name and email fields updates local component state. On blur or on change, validation runs to mark fields as valid or invalid. Invalid fields show error messages and prevent the Place order button from enabling until the issues are resolved.

Selecting a payment method updates local state to a new paymentMethod value and conditionally renders the corresponding payment detail inputs. Switching methods clears or hides irrelevant fields but does not call any backend API until the order is submitted.

Typing into payment detail inputs updates the paymentDetails object in local state. Basic syntactic validation (for example, non-empty values where required) runs locally and influences the enabled or disabled state of the Place order button.

Toggling the Agree to terms checkbox updates a local agreeToTerms boolean. If the box is unchecked, the Place order button remains disabled even if all other fields are valid. If the user tries to submit while it is unchecked (for example, via form submit event), the form prevents submission and highlights the terms section.

Clicking the Place order button or submitting the checkout form triggers a single onSubmit handler, which performs client-side validation for all sections. If validation passes, the handler calls placeOrder with the current purchaserName, purchaserEmail, paymentMethod, paymentDetails, and agreeToTerms values. During the API call, the button shows a loading state and is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

When the placeOrder response returns, success is checked. If success is true, the order-confirmation component is populated with the returned Order and items data, the main view switches to confirmation mode, and header summary components are refreshed by re-calling getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary. If success is false, an error message from response.message is shown in the checkout form, the view remains in checkout mode, and the user can modify inputs and resubmit.

In the confirmation view, the user can review details of each ordered item. If the Order.includesPreorders flag is true, the confirmation component highlights which items are preorders based on OrderItem.isPreorder and may surface releaseDate if available in each OrderItem. No further API calls are required as all needed data is supplied in the placeOrder response.

If the user navigates using the browser back and forward buttons, the page reads the step parameter from the URL on each navigation event to determine whether to show the review/payment form or the confirmation view. The page does not re-call placeOrder based solely on URL changes; only explicit form submission triggers that API.
page_functionality.state_logic: The checkout page uses URL query parameters as the primary mechanism for representing page-level state, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. The main parameter is step, which indicates the current stage of the checkout flow (for example, step=review, step=payment, or step=confirmation). On initial page load, if step is not present or is not recognized, the page defaults to step=review, which shows the order summary and purchaser/payment forms ready for input. When the user completes the review and proceeds to payment (if the implementation distinguishes between these steps), the page updates the URL to set step=payment without reloading, allowing browser navigation to reflect the user's progress. After a successful placeOrder call, the page updates the URL to set step=confirmation to indicate that the primary content should switch to the confirmation view. The confirmation view is powered entirely by the placeOrder response in the current session; the page does not attempt to reconstruct confirmation data solely from the URL on hard refresh because this would require an order lookup API that is not available to this page. If the user clicks Back to cart, the navigation to cart.html removes checkout-specific parameters like step while preserving any unrelated parameters from the original URL. In case of a placeOrder failure that needs to present a high-level error state, the page may optionally append or update an error parameter (for example, error=payment_failed) to reflect that an error is being shown while still remaining on step=payment. Changes to step or error in the URL do not directly trigger any new API calls except the standard reinitialization of getCartSummary, getWishlistSummary, and getCheckoutSummary if the page is fully reloaded. All order submission logic remains tied exclusively to the explicit form submission event rather than URL manipulation.

components.functionality: Displays concise cart and wishlist status in the page header, using data from getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary. Shows the total number of items and pricing summary for the cart, and an item count for the wishlist. Provides a Back to cart control that navigates to cart.html when clicked.

Fetches and displays the detailed checkout summary using getCheckoutSummary. Renders each cart item with its associated product data, differentiates preorder items (based on cartItem.isPreorder and product.availabilityStatus), and lists applied promotions. Shows subtotal, discountTotal, and total from the cart object. Provides contextual messaging if the cart is empty or if preorder items are present.

Collects purchaserName and purchaserEmail required for order creation. Maintains internal state for both fields, applies validation (non-empty, valid email format), and exposes this state to the parent checkout form component. Prevents progression to order submission if validation fails.

Allows the user to select a paymentMethod (card, paypal, or other) and enter method-specific payment details. Maintains an internal representation of paymentMethod and a paymentDetails object which may include method-specific keys. Ensures that all required payment details for the chosen method are present and valid before indicating readiness to place the order.

Displays terms and conditions text and, when preorders are present in the cart, additional preorder-specific information. Provides a required Agree to terms checkbox that controls a boolean agreeToTerms state used when calling placeOrder. If preorders are present as indicated by getCartSummary.hasPreorders or any checkout item with isPreorder true, the section emphasizes preorder behavior and links it logically to the consent requirement.

Coordinates the purchaser-details-form, payment-method-section, and terms-and-preorder-consent components into a single submit action. Gathers purchaserName, purchaserEmail, paymentMethod, paymentDetails, and agreeToTerms from child components. Validates all sections on submit and, if valid, calls placeOrder. Manages loading, success, and error states, and switches the page between review/payment view and confirmation view by updating the step URL parameter.

Displays the confirmation state after a successful placeOrder call. Shows a confirmation message from response.message, key order data (such as orderNumber, total, discountTotal, subtotal, purchaserEmail, paymentMethod, includesPreorders), and a list of ordered items using the returned OrderItem and Product data. Highlights preorder items when Order.includesPreorders is true and OrderItem.isPreorder is true, and uses any available releaseDate fields to explain when preorder ebooks will become available.

Interprets and manages URL query parameters relevant to checkout state, primarily step and optionally error. On initialization, reads the current step value to decide whether to show the checkout-main-form or the order-confirmation-panel. After successful or failed placeOrder responses, updates the step (and optionally error) parameter in the URL so that browser navigation reflects the current stage. Does not itself call any interfaces, but coordinates which components are visible and when they reinitialize their bindings.

layout.layout_pattern: The checkout page uses the same stacked, full-width band structure as the mockup but adapts it for a form-centric flow. Directly beneath the site’s global header/hero area, a slim, full-width header-context bar establishes the checkout context. Below it, the main checkout band is a two-column layout inside a centered max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). The left column is a bold, colored order-summary slab (analogous to the red featured book panel), while the right column is a clean, white form area stacking purchaser details, payment method, and terms/consent in vertical sequence. After submission, the order-confirmation panel replaces this main band but keeps the same centered, editorial presentation.

After submission, the order-confirmation panel replaces this main band but keeps the same centered, editorial presentation.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: About the top 10–15% of the viewport is occupied by the site chrome plus the header-context bar, visually framing the checkout below. The main checkout band then fills most of the remaining viewport height on initial load. Horizontally, the centered content container uses an implied 12-column grid: the left order-summary panel occupies roughly 4 of 12 columns (35–40% width) and the right form column about 8 of 12 columns (60–65% width). Vertical spacing between major sections (context bar, main checkout band, confirmation view) follows the 32–48px rhythm, with 48–64px above and below the main band to maintain a calm, editorial feel despite the density of form fields.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and curated rather than crammed. The order summary emphasizes a small number of items (a few ebooks and preorders) with generous padding, large cover thumbnails, and clear totals. The forms are broken into logical sections with 24–32px spacing between groups and 16–24px between individual fields. Buttons and interactive elements follow the 8px spacing scale, with 12–16px horizontal padding and 12–20px vertical padding to feel comfortably tappable without overwhelming the layout. There is ample breathing room around the main columns to preserve the refined, modern look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the dark header-context bar and centered checkout title, then moves downward into the main band. Within that band, the eye is pulled left to the colored order-summary panel (echoing the red featured block from the mockup), which quickly reassures the user what they are buying. From there, the gaze crosses to the right into the bright, clean form column, scanning top-down through purchaser details, then payment, then terms and the primary action button. On scroll, the order summary remains visually anchored (via sticky behavior on larger screens), while the right-side form content advances step by step. After completion, the visual focus shifts to a confirmation panel with a strong top accent and centered messaging, providing a clear end-state.

layout.overall_description: The checkout page is framed by the site’s dark, minimal chrome at the top and a centered, max-width content band in the middle of the viewport. Immediately under the global navigation, a slim, charcoal header-context bar runs edge to edge, with a succinct “Checkout” title and status cues aligned to the center, echoing the bold announcement bar from the mockup but in a more subdued tone. Below this, the main checkout area forms a horizontal, two-column band that feels like the editorial featured-product section translated into a transactional context. On the left, a richly colored order-summary panel—on a deep red or warm accent background—occupies about a third of the container, visually reminiscent of the red book panel in the mockup. It stacks cover thumbnails and key totals with generous padding, possibly backed by a faint overlay of the bookstore photography to keep the brand’s atmospheric mood. On the right, taking up the remaining width, a clean white form column houses the purchaser details, payment method, and terms/consent sections as distinct but tightly related blocks. Each section uses large, readable typography and clear section headings, separated by 32–40px of vertical space, so the flow feels guided rather than cluttered. A prominent red primary button at the bottom anchors the form. As users scroll, the order summary remains fixed within view on larger screens, reinforcing confidence while the right column’s content advances. On successful completion, the main two-column band is replaced by a single, centered confirmation panel that continues the dark-framed, editorial aesthetic with a bold accent bar, clear success headline, and concise next-step guidance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-context bar sits directly beneath the site’s global navigation/header, spanning the full browser width as a slim, horizontal strip. It uses a charcoal or near-black background to blend with the dark site chrome, with white and subtle red accent text. Inside, content is constrained to the same centered max-width container (about 1100–1200px) as the rest of the page. The bar has approximately 16px of vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding within the container. The main checkout label (e.g., “CHECKOUT”) is centered horizontally with all-caps typography, mirroring the announcement bar hierarchy from the mockup. Secondary information such as a step indicator (e.g., “Step 2 of 2”) may align to the right within the container, while a subtle link back to the cart could align left. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of this bar and the top of the main checkout band, allowing the two-column layout below to breathe and feel distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the scrollable content region (below the global header); does not remain sticky as the user scrolls through the checkout form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-main-form acts as the primary structural band for the page’s transactional content. It begins roughly 32–40px below the header-context bar and extends downward for at least one full viewport height on initial load. This band spans the full width of the browser but centers its actual content within a max-width container (approximately 1100–1200px). Inside this container, the layout splits into a two-column grid that echoes the mockup’s featured-book layout: the left column occupies around 35–40% of the container width (approx. 4 grid columns in a 12-column system) and is reserved exclusively for the checkout-summary-panel; the right column occupies the remaining 60–65% (approx. 8 grid columns) and vertically stacks the purchaser-details-form, payment-method-section, and terms-and-preorder-consent components. Horizontal gutter between columns is about 32px. The overall band has generous top and bottom padding (32–48px) in addition to the container’s internal spacing, reinforcing the editorial, spacious feeling. On tablets and smaller devices, this structure collapses into a single-column layout: the order summary moves above the forms, each component spanning 100% of the container width with 32px vertical spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the main scrollable content; on larger screens, the two-column structure remains fixed while inner panels scroll. On smaller screens, it reflows responsively into a stacked, single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-summary-panel fills the entire left column of the main checkout band, visually mimicking the bold red feature block from the mockup. It occupies roughly 35–40% of the content width and runs from near the top of the form area toward the bottom, with its own internal scroll if necessary. Its background is a solid, warm red or deep accent color, optionally overlaid with a very subtle, low-contrast bookstore photograph texture in the upper portion to echo the hero image’s atmosphere without compromising legibility. Inside, the panel uses generous padding—24–32px on all sides—to maintain a spacious, curated feel. At the top, a concise section title (e.g., “Order Summary”) is set in white, bold text. Below, the ordered ebooks and preorders are listed vertically with cover thumbnails aligned left and text details aligned right within each item row, each row separated by 16px vertical spacing. Totals, taxes, and discounts are grouped near the bottom with stronger typographic hierarchy and a subtle dividing rule. A small lock icon and reassurance copy (“Secure checkout”) can sit near the top or bottom edge, adding trust. On tall viewports, the panel’s visible height roughly matches the viewport height minus the top chrome; on scroll, it remains visually anchored so the user’s order details stay in view alongside the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within its column on desktop and larger tablet screens: the panel remains fixed relative to the viewport after its top edge reaches approximately 96px–120px from the top (to clear the global header and context bar), while the right column forms scroll independently. On small screens, it loses stickiness and becomes a standard block stacked above the forms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchaser-details-form is the first block in the right column of the checkout-main-form, aligned to the top of the column and starting roughly level with the top of the checkout-summary-panel. It sits on a white or very light background, creating a strong contrast against the darker page chrome and the colored summary panel. The form is presented as a single, cohesive card with 24–32px padding on all sides and subtly rounded corners or a faint border to distinguish it from the page background. A section heading (e.g., “Purchaser Details”) appears at the top left of the card, with 8–12px of separation from a short descriptive line (“Used for receipts and account access”). Inside, fields are laid out in a two-column grid where space allows (e.g., first and last name side by side), reverting to a single column on smaller viewports. Vertical spacing between related fields is 12–16px, and between groups (like contact info vs. billing address) is 24px. The overall card is separated from the next section (payment-method-section) by a 32–40px vertical gap, maintaining a clear hierarchy and breathing space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls normally with the rest of the form content. On smaller screens, it spans the full width of the container and appears immediately below the checkout-summary-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-section is placed directly below the purchaser-details-form in the right column, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace. It also appears as a white/light card with similar padding (24–32px) to maintain visual consistency, but with stronger emphasis on security. The top of the card includes a clear heading such as “Payment Method” aligned left, optionally accompanied by small brand marks (card logos) aligned right. Below the heading, the section may begin with radio-style selectors or tabs for payment options (e.g., credit card, PayPal), arranged horizontally within the card width but with enough spacing (16–24px) to feel uncluttered. The active payment method reveals its fields beneath, arranged in a clean, one- or two-column layout depending on viewport width (e.g., card number full-width, expiry and CVC side by side). Input fields are sized to feel comfortably large with 12–16px internal padding and 8–12px spacing between label and input. Error and helper text are tightly associated with each field but kept compact to preserve the overall light density. The bottom of this card sits 24–32px above the terms-and-preorder-consent section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; expands vertically as needed depending on the active payment method, pushing subsequent content downward. On mobile, it spans full width and stacks under the purchaser-details-form with consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-and-preorder-consent section anchors the bottom of the form stack in the right column, positioned approximately 24–32px beneath the payment-method-section. It is visually simpler and slightly more compact than the other cards but still respects the same padding system (20–24px internal padding). The background remains white or light, but typographic emphasis shifts to smaller body text and clear checkboxes. At the top, a concise heading like “Terms & Preorder Consent” is left-aligned, followed by one or more paragraphs of fine-print information in a lighter weight and smaller size. Below each paragraph, corresponding consent controls (e.g., checkboxes acknowledging terms of service, preorder conditions, and cancellation policies) are aligned to the left with labels wrapping to the right, maintaining 8–12px spacing between stacked items. Links to the terms or policies use the site’s accent red, echoing the color used in the order-summary-panel and main CTA. At the bottom of this section, aligned right and extending across the width of the right column, sits the primary action button (e.g., “Place Order”) styled as a bold red, full-width or nearly full-width button with 14–16px vertical padding, creating a strong end-of-flow focal point. The base of this section has 32–48px of spacing before the page footer or any ancillary content, so the CTA isn’t cramped at the fold.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; always appears directly above the page’s bottom spacing or footer. As fields above expand or contract, this section shifts accordingly, keeping the primary CTA at the natural end of the vertical flow. On small screens, it stretches to full container width with the primary button set to full width for finger-friendly tapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-confirmation-panel occupies the same general vertical space as the checkout-main-form but appears after a successful submission, effectively replacing the two-column layout. It is centered within the same max-width content container and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the container width (approx. 7–8 columns in a 12-column grid), leaving generous margins on both sides to heighten the sense of completion and calm. The panel itself is a vertically oriented card with a white or very light background and 32–40px padding. At the very top of the panel, spanning its full width, a horizontal accent band in the brand’s red mirrors the strong color block of the earlier summary panel and the mockup’s red feature rectangle, immediately signaling success. Overlapping or just below this band sits a large confirmation icon (e.g., a simple checkmark in a circle) centered above a bold headline (“Order Confirmed”) and a concise explanation line. Below the primary message, the panel lists key order details (order number, summary of items, total charged) in a lightly bordered or subtly divided area, using 16–24px spacing between rows. Secondary actions such as “Download your ebooks,” “View your library,” or “Back to catalog” align horizontally or stack vertically near the bottom, with a primary next-step button highlighted in red and secondary links in muted text. The panel is vertically centered within the viewport when possible, with 48–64px of top and bottom margin to echo the editorial openness of the hero-driven pages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in place of the checkout-main-form once the state transitions to ‘confirmed’; scrolls normally if its content exceeds the viewport height. Remains centered and does not adopt sticky behavior, emphasizing a single, complete end-state view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-state-router is not directly visible as a UI element but governs which of the main structural components are rendered and how they transition. Conceptually, it wraps the entire content region below the header-context bar. In the ‘review-and-pay’ state, it shows the checkout-main-form (with its two-column layout containing checkout-summary-panel and the stacked form sections). Upon completion and successful payment, it hides the main form and order summary and instead reveals the order-confirmation-panel in the same central content region. In error or retry states, it maintains the two-column layout but surfaces inline alerts at the top of the right form column, just below the header-context bar. The router ensures that spatial relationships remain consistent across states—content always appears centered in the max-width container with similar top and bottom margins—so transitions feel like a calm evolution of the same page rather than a jarring navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical controller only; it dictates component visibility and may trigger smooth opacity/slide transitions when swapping between the checkout-main-form and order-confirmation-panel, but it does not occupy its own physical space in the layout.

name: Wishlist
title: Wishlist
description: Page for viewing and managing ebooks saved for later, including moving items to the cart, removing items, and navigating to full product details.

core_features: Load and display the current wishlist using getWishlistDetails, showing each ebook with key information from the Product and WishlistItem data.
Show the total number of wishlist items using getWishlistSummary and keep the header wishlist indicator in sync with changes on this page.
Show a cart summary (item count and totals) using getCartSummary so users can see the impact of moving wishlist items to the cart.
Allow users to remove individual ebooks from the wishlist using removeFromWishlist.
Allow users to move a wishlist item to the cart and simultaneously remove it from the wishlist using moveWishlistItemToCart with removeFromWishlist = true.
Allow users to add a wishlist item to the cart while keeping it in the wishlist using moveWishlistItemToCart with removeFromWishlist = false.
Provide per-item navigation to the product detail page (product.html) by clicking the ebook title or cover, passing id and returnUrl=wishlist.html via URL parameters.
Provide navigation to the cart page (cart.html) after items are moved to the cart, via a dedicated 'View cart' action.
Provide navigation to the browse page (browse.html?categoryId=all_ebooks) so users can continue exploring ebooks when done with the wishlist or when it is empty.
