Vertical padding matches the header’s overall vertical rhythm (about 12–16px top and bottom inside its clickable area), keeping it visually balanced with adjacent items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Like the cart, the wishlist widget is part of the sticky header and remains fixed at the top as the user scrolls. It maintains its relative position immediately to the left of the cart widget on large screens. On smaller screens with a compressed or icon-only header, the wishlist may collapse to an icon-only representation but stays in proximity to the cart, preserving a logical grouping of shopping-related summaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support navigation list is visually embedded inside the About content section’s left red column rather than existing as a standalone band. Within that red panel, the list is positioned in the middle-to-lower half, starting roughly 24–32px below any About heading or mission statement text. It is left-aligned and constrained to the same 24–32px internal padding from the panel’s left edge. The list consists of 3–6 vertically stacked links (e.g., ‘Help Center’, ‘Contact Us’, ‘FAQs’, ‘Shipping & Returns’), each separated by 8–16px of vertical space to maintain readability and a calm, editorial rhythm. Each item is a simple text link in white (or very light text) contrasting against the red background, with subtle underline or weight change on hover. The list has a small section label above it, such as ‘Need Help?’ set in a slightly smaller uppercase style, reinforcing a clear hierarchy. The right text column of the About section aligns its top heading horizontally roughly with the top of this support block, so when viewed together, the red left column anchors support and quick access actions while the right column tells the brand story. On mobile, when the columns stack, this navigation list appears directly under the left column’s About heading and mission statement and above any spacing that separates this block from the main text block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The support navigation list scrolls with the About section content and does not have sticky behavior. Its position is anchored relative to the red panel inside the About section; as the section resizes vertically with dynamic content, the list maintains its internal padding and vertical spacing, always remaining comfortably away from the panel’s bottom edge (at least 24–32px of space). On narrow screens, it adjusts to the full width of the stacked red panel while preserving its vertical spacing.

name: Help & FAQ
title: Help & FAQ
description: Provides dynamic help and frequently asked questions for using the ebook store, including guidance on searching, purchasing, preordering, downloading, and managing cart and wishlist, with quick access to contact support.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries via getFaqEntries and display them in a filterable, searchable list.
Pre-filter FAQ entries by category using the incoming `category` URL parameter (e.g., `search_filter`, `purchasing`, `preorders`, `downloads`, `promo_codes`, `wishlist`).
Allow users to change the active FAQ category on the page; update the visible FAQ entries and synchronize the `category` URL parameter without a full page reload.
Provide a client-side FAQ search field that filters the currently loaded FAQ entries by matching text in their `question` and `answer` fields.
Enable expand/collapse interaction for each FAQ entry so users can reveal or hide answers individually.
Show a header summary of the current cart by calling getCartSummary, displaying item count, subtotal, discountTotal, total, and a preorder indicator.
Show a header summary of the wishlist by calling getWishlistSummary, displaying the wishlist item count.
Offer a clear 'Contact support' action that navigates the user to contact.html when FAQs are not sufficient.
user_workflows:
Accessing Help & FAQ with contextual category pre-filter:
1. User clicks a help link on another page (e.g., cart or checkout) that navigates to help.html with a `category` URL parameter.
2. Help & FAQ page loads and reads the `category` parameter from the URL.
3. Page calls getFaqEntries once and receives an array of FAQ entries with their `category` values.
4. Page sets the active category filter to the value from the URL, if it matches at least one entry's `category`.
5. FAQ list displays only entries matching the active category; other categories remain available in the on-page category selector.
6. User can change category on the page; the FAQ list updates and the `category` parameter in the URL is updated accordingly.

Browsing FAQs directly from footer navigation:
1. User clicks 'Help & FAQ' in the footer and navigates to help.html without a `category` parameter.
2. On load, page calls getFaqEntries.
3. Page initializes the active category state to an unfiltered view (e.g., 'all') because no `category` parameter is present.
4. All FAQ entries returned by getFaqEntries are visible, optionally grouped or labeled by their `category`.
5. User may then choose a specific category from the category filter component; the URL is updated to include `category=<selectedCategory>`.
6. The FAQ list re-renders to show only entries whose `category` matches the selected value.

Searching within FAQs:
1. After FAQs are loaded via getFaqEntries, the user types into the FAQ search input.
2. On each search submission or change event (depending on configuration), the page takes the current active category and search query.
3. The FAQ list is filtered client-side to only show entries whose `question` or `answer` text matches the search query, while still respecting the active category filter.
4. If no entries match the combination of category and query, the page shows a dynamic empty-state message based on the search term and current category (text is not hardcoded to specific FAQs).
5. The user clears or modifies the search query to broaden or refine the visible FAQs.

Reading FAQ answers and toggling details:
1. User sees a list of FAQ questions corresponding to the active category and current search query.
2. User clicks on a question header row.
3. The page toggles an 'expanded' state for that FAQ entry; if expanded, the `answer` text from getFaqEntries is shown; if collapsed, it is hidden.
4. The user can expand multiple FAQ entries or collapse them individually; this state is kept client-side and is independent of the URL parameters.

Using header cart and wishlist information while on Help & FAQ:
1. When the page loads, in parallel with getFaqEntries, the page calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary.
2. The header area displays cart summary data (itemCount, subtotal, discountTotal, total, hasPreorders) and wishlist summary data (itemCount) using the returned objects.
3. If the user clicks the cart icon or summary area, the page navigates to the cart page URL (handled outside this spec) while preserving any existing query parameters unrelated to Help & FAQ, if desired.
4. If the user clicks the wishlist icon or summary area, the page navigates to the wishlist page URL (handled outside this spec).

Escalating from FAQ to contact support:
1. User reads several FAQs and still needs assistance.
2. User clicks a 'Contact support' link or button located near the top or bottom of the FAQ list.
3. The page performs a navigation to contact.html.
4. Optionally, the link can preserve the current `category` parameter or append a new parameter to contact.html to indicate the originating context (the exact parameters are to be defined centrally, but Help & FAQ does not rely on them).
interactions:
Category selection:
- Interaction: Click on a category control (e.g., tab, pill, or dropdown option) representing a FAQ category code.
- Effect: Updates the active category state in the page, updates the `category` query parameter in the URL to the selected category code, and re-filters the FAQ list to only show entries where entry.category equals the selected category. Does not call getFaqEntries again unless a full page reload occurs.

URL-driven category state on load or navigation:
- Interaction: Browser loads help.html or the query string changes (e.g., via back/forward navigation to a previous category).
- Effect: Page reads the `category` parameter from the URL. If it matches at least one FAQ entry's `category`, the page sets that as the active category. If it does not match any entry, the page falls back to an unfiltered view or a default category without altering the displayed data returned by getFaqEntries.

FAQ search input:
- Interaction: User types a search query into a text input and either presses enter or triggers a search button.
- Effect: Page sets an internal searchQuery state and filters the in-memory FAQ entries from getFaqEntries by checking if question or answer text contains the search term (case-handling behavior is defined by the implementation). The filtering respects the active category. URL parameters are not changed by search (search state is kept in-memory).

FAQ item expand/collapse:
- Interaction: User clicks on an FAQ question row or an explicit expand/collapse control.
- Effect: Component toggles an internal boolean state (e.g., isExpanded) for that specific FAQ entry. When isExpanded is true, the associated answer text is rendered; when false, it is hidden. This does not affect any other FAQ entries and is not stored in URL parameters.

Contact support navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks a 'Contact support' link or button on the Help & FAQ page.
- Effect: Browser navigates to contact.html as specified in the outgoing connections. No additional API calls are required for this action.

Header cart summary interaction:
- Interaction: On page load, getCartSummary is called without user input.
- Effect: The header cart component binds itemCount, subtotal, discountTotal, total, and hasPreorders from the response and displays them. If the user clicks the header cart area, the browser navigates to the cart page (URL defined at the application level).

Header wishlist summary interaction:
- Interaction: On page load, getWishlistSummary is called without user input.
- Effect: The header wishlist component binds itemCount from the response and displays it. If the user clicks the header wishlist area, the browser navigates to the wishlist page (URL defined at the application level).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL-driven state is the `category` query parameter. On initial page load and whenever the browser's location changes (including back/forward navigation), the Help & FAQ page reads `category` from the URL query string. After getFaqEntries resolves, the page evaluates whether the `category` value corresponds to at least one entry.category in the returned dataset. If it does, the page sets an internal activeCategory state to that value and filters the visible FAQ list accordingly. If no FAQ entries use that category value or the parameter is absent, the page sets activeCategory to an internal neutral value (e.g., representing 'no category filter applied') and displays all FAQ entries. Changes initiated from the UI (e.g., user selecting another category) update both the internal activeCategory state and the URL's `category` parameter, using history APIs that do not trigger a full page reload. Search queries and FAQ expand/collapse states are maintained purely in client-side state and are not synchronized to the URL, so reloading the page or navigating away will reset them. Calls to getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary are not affected by the `category` parameter; they run independently on every page load to ensure header information remains synchronized with the user's current cart and wishlist.

components.functionality: Initializes the Help & FAQ page, orchestrates loading FAQ, cart, and wishlist summaries, reads and applies the `category` URL parameter, manages global page state (activeCategory, searchQuery), and renders child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Triggered when help.html is loaded; calls getFaqEntries, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary in parallel, then initializes internal state based on the URL `category` parameter and fetched FAQ data.

onUrlChange: Triggered when the browser history changes (e.g., back/forward navigation); re-reads the `category` query parameter and updates activeCategory, then re-applies filters to the already loaded FAQ entries without re-calling getFaqEntries.

onCategoryChange: Receives a new category value from the faq-category-filter component, updates activeCategory state, filters the FAQ data, and updates the `category` query parameter using pushState/replaceState.
onSearchChange: Receives search query updates from the faq-search-form, updates searchQuery state, and re-filters the in-memory FAQ entries for the faq-list component.
onContactSupportClick: Handles event from faq-contact-support-cta and navigates the browser to contact.html.

components.functionality: Displays concise cart and wishlist summaries in the page header using getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary, allowing users to see their current item counts and totals while reading Help & FAQ.
event_handlers: onCartSummaryDataLoad: Invoked when getCartSummary resolves; binds itemCount, subtotal, discountTotal, total, and hasPreorders to the header cart display.
onWishlistSummaryDataLoad: Invoked when getWishlistSummary resolves; binds itemCount to the header wishlist display.
onCartIconClick: Navigates the user from help.html to the cart page URL defined at the application level.
onWishlistIconClick: Navigates the user from help.html to the wishlist page URL defined at the application level.

components.functionality: Provides controls for selecting which FAQ category is active, including reflecting any `category` value present in the URL and allowing the user to switch categories on the page.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the activeCategory state from the help-page-shell and visually marks the corresponding category as selected.
onCategorySelect: Triggered when the user selects a category option; sends the selected category code to the help-page-shell via onCategoryChange so the FAQ list and URL parameter are updated.

components.functionality: Allows users to input a free-text query to filter FAQ entries by question or answer text within the currently active category.
data_binding: FaqEntry (question and answer fields from getFaqEntries)
event_handlers: onSearchInputChange: Updates a local representation of the search query as the user types; optionally debounces the event.
onSearchSubmit: Sends the current search query to help-page-shell via onSearchChange for centralized filtering of the FAQ list.
onSearchClear: Resets the search input and signals help-page-shell to clear the searchQuery state, restoring the full list for the current category.

components.functionality: Renders the collection of FAQ entries based on the active category and search query, and manages per-entry expansion state so users can read answers.
data_binding: activeCategory (derived from URL and user interactions)
event_handlers: onDataBind: Receives the filtered list of FAQ entries from help-page-shell whenever activeCategory or searchQuery changes; re-renders the list.
onFaqItemToggle: Triggered when a specific FAQ entry is expanded or collapsed; updates that entry's local isExpanded state without changing URL parameters or re-calling getFaqEntries.

components.functionality: Represents a single FAQ entry with its question always visible and its answer shown or hidden based on expansion state.
event_handlers: onClickQuestion: Toggles the isExpanded state for this FAQ item and notifies faq-list via onFaqItemToggle.
onKeyboardToggle: Allows keyboard users to expand or collapse the item (e.g., with Enter or Space when focused), mirroring onClickQuestion behavior.

components.functionality: Provides a clear call-to-action for users who need assistance beyond the FAQs, linking them to the contact.html page.
event_handlers: onClickContactSupport: When the user activates the contact support control, this handler delegates to help-page-shell's onContactSupportClick, which triggers navigation to contact.html.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top‑to‑bottom structure: a slim announcement bar (outside this spec), then the global navigation/header (containing the cart/wishlist summary), then a full-bleed hero image introducing Help & FAQ. Below the hero, a prominent two-column band mirrors the featured product pattern: a bold, colored support block on the left and a clean, white help-tools area on the right. Under this band, a vertically stacked FAQ list runs down the page. All content sits inside a centered, max-width container aligned to an implied 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top‑to‑bottom structure: a slim announcement bar (outside this spec), then the global navigation/header (containing the cart/wishlist summary), then a full-bleed hero image introducing Help & FAQ. Below the hero, a prominent two-column band mirrors the featured product pattern: a bold, colored support block on the left and a clean, white help-tools area on the right. Under this band, a vertically stacked FAQ list runs down the page. All content sits inside a centered, max-width container aligned to an implied 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height is the announcement + header zone. The hero image occupies about 45–55% of the first viewport, leaving the top of the help tools band visible at the fold. The two-column help tools band extends 30–40% of the viewport height with a 35–40% (left) / 60–65% (right) width split. The FAQ list beneath uses the remaining vertical space, extending as long as needed. Horizontally, most components stay within a 1100–1200px centered container, with full-bleed treatment reserved for header and hero.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious: header controls are compact but not cramped; the hero is visually rich with minimal overlaid text; the two-column help band uses large typography and generous padding; and FAQ items are laid out as roomy, accordion-style rows rather than dense lines of text. There are 32–48px vertical gaps between sections and 16–24px internal padding in cards and list items, maintaining an airy, editorial feel while still surfacing a good number of questions per screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the dark announcement/header band, moves to the central logo, then over to the right where the cart/wishlist summary lives. From there, it drops into the full-bleed hero image with a Help & FAQ title. As the user scrolls, attention is pulled to the left-hand bold, red support block, then across to the right-hand white help tools area with the search form and category filters. From the search, the gaze flows downward into the structured FAQ list, following a Z-like pattern that alternates between strong color anchors on the left and clean text areas on the right and below.

layout.overall_description: The Help & FAQ page sits inside the same dark, image-driven shell as the store’s homepage. A slim black announcement bar and a charcoal navigation header anchor the top, framing the viewport against a full-bleed, atmospheric bookstore photograph that introduces the Help & FAQ context with minimal overlaid text. Below the hero, the main content shifts into a centered, max-width band. The first band echoes the featured-product layout: a bold red block on the left, dedicated to quick support and reassurance, faces a bright white panel on the right where a large “How can we help?” heading, a wide search bar, and horizontal category filters live. Together they form a visually strong, two-column toolset that encourages either searching or browsing by topic. Beneath this, the layout relaxes into a single, vertically stacked FAQ list with spacious, accordion-style questions that stretch across most of the container width. Throughout, the design relies on clear hierarchy—dark chrome framing, strong color on the left, large type on the right, then a calm list below—to guide users intuitively from global navigation to finding specific answers or reaching support.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell defines a full-viewport canvas with a dark chrome top and content centered within a max-width container of approximately 1100–1200px. At the very top, spanning the entire browser width, is a slim black announcement bar (around 40px tall) with a centered, all-caps message. Immediately below, a charcoal header bar (roughly 72–88px tall) runs full-width and contains the navigation and logo aligned to the central content grid. The header and announcement bar form the top 10–15% of the viewport. Below the header, the hero region fills 45–55% of the initial viewport height, with a full-bleed bookstore photo extending edge to edge; its textual overlay (page title and short description) stays aligned with the central content column, roughly centered vertically within the hero. The main Help content begins 48–64px below the hero overlay, inside the centered container. Here, the first block is a two-column band that spans the full width of the container: the left 35–40% is a solid red panel, flush to the left edge of the container; the right 60–65% is a white or very light panel aligned to the right. Beneath this band, separated by about 48px of vertical space, the FAQ list stretches down the page, occupying about 80% of the container width and centered to maintain comfortable side margins. The page shell maintains 32–48px vertical spacing between major sections and consistent 16–24px internal padding within panels and cards, preserving a calm, editorial rhythm across desktop. On smaller screens, the two-column band stacks vertically, with the red support block appearing above the white search/filter area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static page wrapper; header and hero scroll out of view normally (no fixed positioning by default). The main content container remains centered and fluid up to its max width; below that, it uses full-width with 16–24px side padding on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart and wishlist summary sits within the global navigation/header bar, nested in the rightmost third of the central header content. Inside the charcoal header strip, the content is divided into three logical zones aligned to the 12-column grid: primary nav links on the left 30%, the logo centered across the middle 40%, and utilities on the right 30%. The header-cart-wishlist-summary occupies the right side of this utilities zone, aligned horizontally with the search icon or field to its left. Visually, it appears as a compact horizontal grouping of icons and labels (e.g., a heart and cart icon with small count tags) aligned to the right edge of the content container, with about 16–24px padding above and below for breathing room. The elements are spaced with 8–16px gaps so they feel touchable but not crowded. On larger screens, this summary aligns vertically centered within the header bar and sits flush with the top edge of the hero image below, reinforcing the sense of a continuous top chrome. On smaller screens, it can collapse into icons-only, still anchored to the upper-right corner of the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and anchored within the header; it scrolls away with the header. On narrow viewports, it may be grouped into a compact icon cluster aligned to the right, but it does not detach or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ category filter is positioned within the right-hand white panel of the two-column help tools band, directly below the main help search form. The right panel itself occupies about 60–65% of the content container width and is aligned to the right edge of that container. Inside this panel, a large heading (“How can we help?” or similar) sits at the top with roughly 24–32px top padding. Beneath the heading, the search form spans most of the panel width. Immediately below the search, separated by about 16px of vertical space, the faq-category-filter appears as a horizontal row (or two) of pill-shaped category chips or segmented buttons aligned to the left edge of the panel’s content area. The filter area occupies the full width of the panel but typically around 1–2 rows tall (48–96px), with 8–16px gaps between individual category pills. The bottom of this filter section has about 24–32px padding before the edge of the white panel, ensuring it feels contained as part of the tools band. On smaller screens, when the two columns stack, the category filter follows directly under the search field in a single-column stack and may allow horizontal scrolling for overflow categories while remaining visually tied to the search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right panel; on mobile it becomes a horizontally scrollable chip row if categories overflow, but remains in-place under the search form without floating or sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search form is the focal point of the right-hand white help panel within the two-column band. The right panel (about 60–65% width) contains a large, bold heading at the top, followed by a short explanatory line. Directly beneath this copy, centered horizontally within the panel but aligned to the left edge of the internal content grid, the search form spans approximately 70–80% of the panel width, leaving comfortable margins on both sides. It sits roughly in the upper-middle of the panel height, with 24–32px padding above and below to maintain an airy, hero-like feel. The search input itself is a long, single-line field with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, visually echoing the wide search control in the main site header but slightly larger to signal its importance. A search icon sits on the left or right end inside the field. The entire form area (input + any helper text or filters label) occupies about 96–128px of vertical height within the right panel. On wider screens, the search bar is the first strong element the eye hits inside the white panel when scanning from the red left block across to the right, anchoring the user’s attention. On small screens, the search form becomes full-width within the stacked white panel, still positioned immediately below the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right panel; no sticky behavior. It reflows to full-width inside the main content column on smaller viewports while retaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list lives below the two-column help tools band, providing a clear transition from tools to detailed answers. It starts approximately 48–64px beneath the bottom edge of the two-column band, maintaining the page’s generous section spacing. The list is contained within the same centered max-width container as the band but uses a slightly narrower text column (about 70–80% of the container width) to keep line lengths readable. This column is centered horizontally, leaving equal white space on both sides for an editorial feel. At the top of the FAQ list, an optional small heading such as “Frequently asked questions” can sit aligned to the left edge of the FAQ column, with 16–24px of space below it before the first item. FAQ items are then stacked vertically in a single column, each occupying the full width of the FAQ column. There is 8–16px of vertical gap between items, with each item’s own interior padding (16–24px) forming clear, card-like rows. The list can extend down the page as needed; the consistent left alignment and grid adherence keep it visually tied to the help tools area above. On smaller screens, the FAQ list simply becomes a full-width column with 16–24px side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list; it scrolls normally with the page. It does not fix or paginate by default, but can be dynamically filtered or collapsed based on the search and category filter interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each faq-item is a spacious, accordion-style row within the FAQ list. It spans the full width of the FAQ text column (about 70–80% of the overall container on desktop) and is left-aligned with the list heading. The item appears as a clean, white or very light card on the default page background, with 16–24px padding on all sides and a subtle border or divider line at the bottom to separate it from the next item. The top of the item contains the question text in a slightly larger, bold font, aligned to the left, occupying roughly the left 80–90% of the item width. On the far right, within the remaining 10–20% width, a minimal expansion indicator (e.g., a chevron icon) is vertically centered with the question text to visually hint at its collapsible nature. In its collapsed state, the item height is driven by the question text plus padding (typically 56–80px tall). When expanded, the answer text slides open beneath the question, indented slightly (e.g., 8–16px) or aligned flush, and uses a comfortable line height with about 8–12px of spacing above and below paragraphs. The expanded content can take up an additional 48–120px in height depending on answer length. There is an 8–16px vertical gap between consecutive faq-items, preserving the airy, editorial rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the FAQ list; the item expands and collapses in place without shifting position in the viewport. On small screens, all content within an item flows into a simple single-column layout with the chevron right-aligned on the same row as the question.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact support CTA is housed within the left-hand red support block of the two-column help tools band, making it a strong, early focal point after the hero. This red panel occupies about 35–40% of the container width and runs the full height of the band. Inside it, the content aligns to the same vertical grid as the right panel, with generous padding (24–32px) on all sides. The upper portion of the red block may contain a short reassurance heading and a brief description, while the faq-contact-support-cta itself appears as a prominent button-style link or compact card anchored in the lower half of the panel. The CTA is horizontally left-aligned within the panel’s content area and typically spans about 50–70% of the panel’s width, providing a comfortable clickable area without reaching edge-to-edge. There is approximately 16–24px of space above and below the CTA so it doesn’t feel cramped. On tall viewports, the CTA may appear slightly lower than center, reinforcing the left-to-right, top-down reading flow (hero title -> tools -> support). On smaller screens, when the two columns stack, the red support block (with the CTA) appears above the white search/filter panel, keeping support highly visible before users reach the search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left red panel; it does not float or stick. Its position within the panel is vertically flexible (can be center- or bottom-weighted) but always maintains consistent interior padding and alignment on all screen sizes.

name: Contact
title: Contact Support
description: Contact Support page where users can send support requests or questions, view support contact details, and navigate to self-service Help/FAQ resources.

core_features: Display dynamic support contact information (support email, business address if available, and support hours) using getSupportInfo.
Provide a contact form with fields for name, email, subject, and message, submitting support requests via submitContactRequest.
Pre-fill the contact form subject field from the optional subject URL parameter when coming from help.html.
Show success or error feedback after submitting the contact form while staying on contact.html.
Provide a link from the Contact page to help.html so users can access FAQs before or instead of submitting a request.
Display cart and wishlist summaries in the page header using getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary for consistent e-commerce context.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arriving on the Contact page and seeing support info:
1. User opens contact.html (from footer or another page).
2. On page load, the page calls getSupportInfo.
3. When getSupportInfo resolves, the support email, business address (if provided), and support hours are displayed in the support information component.
4. In parallel, the page calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to populate header indicators for cart and wishlist counts.

Workflow 2 – Pre-filled subject from Help/FAQ:
1. User is on help.html and clicks a link such as "Contact support about this topic".
2. The user is navigated to contact.html with a subject URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=<encoded-subject-from-FAQ>).
3. On initial load of contact.html, the Contact page reads the subject query parameter.
4. If a subject query parameter exists, the contact form initializes the subject field with that value.
5. The user can keep this pre-filled text or edit it freely; subsequent user edits override the initial URL value in the in-page state.

Workflow 3 – Submitting a new support request:
1. User reviews the support information and then fills in the contact form fields: name, email, subject (pre-filled or manual), and message.
2. As the user types, the form updates its internal state for each field and clears any field-specific validation messages when the user corrects input.
3. When the user submits the form, client-side validation runs (for example, ensuring required fields are not empty and email appears valid) without using hardcoded copy for messages.
4. If validation fails, the form displays appropriate validation indications associated with the relevant fields and does not call submitContactRequest.
5. If validation passes, the form enters a submitting state (e.g., disabling the submit button) and calls submitContactRequest with the current values for name, email, subject, and message.
6. When submitContactRequest returns, the form exits the submitting state.
7. If the response indicates success, the page remains on contact.html, displays a confirmation/feedback banner including the returned ticketId (if present) and message from the API, and may optionally reset the form fields according to product requirements.
8. If the response indicates failure, the page displays an error state/feedback banner using the message returned by the API and allows the user to modify the form and resubmit.

Workflow 4 – Navigating to Help/FAQ from the Contact page:
1. User is on contact.html and is unsure whether to submit a support request.
2. The user clicks the "View FAQs" or equivalent link provided on the Contact page.
3. The page navigates to help.html without additional URL parameters (as defined in outgoing navigation).
4. The user can later return to contact.html via site navigation or from contextual FAQ links that may include a subject parameter.

Workflow 5 – Interacting with cart and wishlist summaries in the header:
1. On contact.html load, header components call getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary.
2. When these calls resolve, the header shows the current cart item count and total, and wishlist item count.
3. If the user clicks the cart icon, they are navigated according to the global site behavior (e.g., to cart.html), without altering the Contact page state.
4. If the user clicks the wishlist icon, they are navigated according to the global site behavior (e.g., to wishlist.html), without altering the Contact page state.
interactions: On page load, the Contact page triggers calls to getSupportInfo, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary to initialize support info and header summaries.
Reading the subject query parameter from the URL and using it to initialize the subject field of the contact form on first render.
User typing into the contact form fields (name, email, subject, message), which updates internal form state and clears any field-level validation indicators for each changed field.
Form validation on submit, blocking submission and highlighting invalid fields when required values are missing or invalid, without calling submitContactRequest.
Form submission event that calls submitContactRequest with the current form state when validation passes, and a corresponding loading/disabling state while awaiting the response.
Displaying a confirmation or error banner component based on the submitContactRequest response (using success, ticketId, and message), while staying on contact.html.
User clicking a "View FAQs" interaction element that navigates to help.html.
Header cart icon interaction that, after showing item counts via getCartSummary, allows navigation to the cart page according to global navigation rules.
Header wishlist icon interaction that, after showing item counts via getWishlistSummary, allows navigation to the wishlist page according to global navigation rules.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page state is driven by URL parameters, interface responses, and transient in-memory state, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage.

URL parameters:
- subject (string): When contact.html is loaded, the page reads the subject query parameter, if present. If the form has not yet been interacted with, the subject field is initialized with this value. Once the user edits the subject field, further changes to the URL (e.g., via navigation) are only applied on full page reload. No additional URL parameters are introduced by this page; success/error states are kept in memory.

Support info state:
- On initial load, the page enters a 'supportInfoLoading' state and calls getSupportInfo.
- When getSupportInfo resolves successfully, 'supportInfoLoading' is set to false, and support info values (supportEmail, businessAddress, supportHours) are stored in state and bound to the support info component.
- If getSupportInfo fails, the page enters a 'supportInfoError' state; the component can display an error indication or fallback message defined outside this specification.

Form state:
- The contact form maintains in-memory state for fields: name, email, subject, and message, plus per-field validation flags and messages defined outside this specification.
- A 'hasUserEditedSubject' flag distinguishes between pre-filled subject from the URL parameter and user-entered text, so URL-based pre-fill only occurs once on initial render.
- A 'isSubmitting' state flag prevents multiple concurrent submissions and allows the UI to disable the submit action while submitContactRequest is in progress.
- A 'submissionResult' state object holds the latest response from submitContactRequest, including success, ticketId, and message. This state is used by the submission feedback component to render a confirmation or error state.

Header summary state:
- On load, the header triggers getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary and stores their responses in 'cartSummary' and 'wishlistSummary' state objects, containing the fields returned by the interfaces.
- If these calls fail, header components can display a neutral fallback state (e.g., hiding counts or showing an undefined count) using UI copy defined outside this specification.

Navigation state:
- Navigating to help.html from the Contact page is a direct link and does not modify the Contact page’s internal state beyond causing a page change.
- Returning to contact.html from other pages re-initializes the state according to the rules above, including re-reading any subject query parameter.

components.functionality: Provides the overall structural container for the Contact page, initializes global header elements (including cart and wishlist summaries), and embeds the support info panel, contact form, submission feedback banner, and FAQ link block.
event_handlers: onInit: calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary and passes results to header components.
onRouteChange: when navigating to contact.html, reads the subject URL parameter and forwards it as initial state to the contact-form component.

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the current shopping cart in the page header, such as item count and relevant totals, for user awareness while on the Contact page.
data_binding: CartSummary (itemCount, subtotal, discountTotal, total, hasPreorders) from getCartSummary
event_handlers: onInit: receives CartSummary data from contact-layout-shell after getCartSummary resolves.
onClick: navigates to the cart page according to global navigation rules (e.g., cart.html), without modifying Contact page-specific state.

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the wishlist in the page header, such as total items, for user awareness while on the Contact page.
event_handlers: onInit: receives WishlistSummary data from contact-layout-shell after getWishlistSummary resolves.
onClick: navigates to the wishlist page according to global navigation rules (e.g., wishlist.html), without modifying Contact page-specific state.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays support contact information for the user, such as support email, business address if provided, and support hours, enabling users to choose between emailing directly or using the form.
event_handlers: onInit: called within contact-layout-shell to trigger getSupportInfo.
onSupportInfoLoaded: updates the displayed support contact details when getSupportInfo resolves.
onSupportInfoError: updates the component to display an error indication if getSupportInfo fails.

components.functionality: Collects user details (name, email) and inquiry information (subject, message), supports initialization of the subject field from the subject URL parameter, validates input, and submits a support request via submitContactRequest.
event_handlers: onInit: reads the subject URL parameter (provided by contact-layout-shell) and initializes the subject field if present and not yet edited by the user.
onChangeName: updates FormState.name and clears validation indicators related to the name field.
onChangeEmail: updates FormState.email and clears validation indicators related to the email field.
onChangeSubject: updates FormState.subject, sets HasUserEditedSubject to true, and clears validation indicators related to the subject field.
onChangeMessage: updates FormState.message and clears validation indicators related to the message field.
onValidate: checks that required fields are populated and that email is syntactically valid; updates FormValidationState without calling submitContactRequest if validation fails.
onSubmit: triggered when the user submits the form; runs onValidate and, if valid, sets SubmissionState.isSubmitting to true and calls submitContactRequest(name, email, subject, message).

onSubmitSuccess: triggered when submitContactRequest returns with success=true; sets SubmissionState.isSubmitting to false, updates SubmissionResult with success, ticketId, and message, and optionally resets FormState according to product rules.

onSubmitError: triggered when submitContactRequest returns with success=false or an error; sets SubmissionState.isSubmitting to false and updates SubmissionResult with success=false and the message from the response.

components.functionality: Displays a success confirmation or error message after a contact request is submitted, using the result of submitContactRequest, while keeping the user on contact.html.
event_handlers: onSubmissionResultChange: listens for changes in SubmissionResult from the contact-form component and updates banner content and styling accordingly.
onDismiss: allows the user to dismiss the banner, which clears or hides the current SubmissionResult state while preserving any form data.

components.functionality: Provides an explicit navigation link from the Contact page to the Help/FAQ page (help.html) so users can seek self-service answers before or after contacting support.
event_handlers: onClickViewFaqs: navigates the user to help.html with no additional URL parameters when the FAQ link is clicked.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same stacked, full-width sequence as the mockup: a slim announcement bar at the very top, followed by a single-row global header with three segments (left nav, center logo, right utilities), then a full-bleed hero band with the atmospheric bookstore image and a prominent “Contact Support” heading. Below the hero, the main contact area is a two-column block that echoes the featured book layout: a bold, red left panel for support info, and a clean, white right panel for the contact form. Beneath this, a full-width or container-aligned FAQ/self‑service band concludes the page, mirroring the site’s pattern of horizontal content bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same stacked, full-width sequence as the mockup: a slim announcement bar at the very top, followed by a single-row global header with three segments (left nav, center logo, right utilities), then a full-bleed hero band with the atmospheric bookstore image and a prominent “Contact Support” heading. Below the hero, the main contact area is a two-column block that echoes the featured book layout: a bold, red left panel for support info, and a clean, white right panel for the contact form. Beneath this, a full-width or container-aligned FAQ/self‑service band concludes the page, mirroring the site’s pattern of horizontal content bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header (announcement + nav) to roughly 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero image occupies about 35–45% of the initial viewport height on this page (slightly shorter than a homepage hero but still immersive), with enough depth to show the bookstore environment behind a concise hero message. The main two-column contact section sits immediately below the hero, inside a centered content container (max-width ~1100–1200px), with the red support panel taking about 35–40% of the width and the contact form about 60–65%. The FAQ/self-service band uses the full content width and is separated by generous vertical spacing (48–64px) to preserve the editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious, curated feeling. Header items and indicators are clearly separated, with 12–16px horizontal padding between interactive elements. The hero contains minimal text layered over rich photography, keeping UI elements light. In the main section, the red support panel uses large, simple typography and a short list of key contact details; the contact form is clean with comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px between fields). The FAQ block offers a concise set of self‑service links rather than a dense list, preserving the store’s boutique, editorial tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye along a Z-shaped path adapted for support: the user first notices the high-contrast black announcement bar message at the top, then the center logo in the header, then moves rightward to the cart and wishlist summaries. From there, the gaze drops into the hero image and the prominent 'Contact Support' heading, anchored slightly left of center. As the user scrolls, the vivid red support info panel on the left becomes the next focal point; from that anchor, the eye moves to the right into the white contact form area where interaction happens. Finally, the flow continues downward into the FAQ/self‑service band, which gathers secondary help options in a calm, low-friction block.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits within the same dark, image-driven shell as the rest of the bookstore: a slim black announcement bar and a charcoal navigation header frame the top, followed immediately by a full-bleed photograph of a cozy bookstore. Over the hero image, a restrained, high-contrast 'Contact Support' title and short subline float near the lower-left of the image, inviting the user to scroll down for help. Below the hero, the main content snaps into a centered, 12-column grid. On desktop, a bold red panel occupies the left third to two-fifths of the content width, holding the essential support details; to its right, a slightly wider white panel contains the contact form, with fields stacked vertically and ample breathing room. If a submission has occurred, a slim feedback banner appears at the top of this right-hand column. The whole block feels like a featured editorial card: strong color on the left, calm whitespace on the right. Below this primary contact area, a horizontally banded FAQ section runs across the page with a light background tint, offering a small set of prominent links to self‑service help content. Spacing between these sections is generous, making the page feel deliberate and uncluttered while still clearly signaling where to act and where to explore further.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The layout shell defines the overall page frame and grid. At the very top of the viewport, a full-width black announcement bar spans 100% of the page width with a height of about 40px (roughly 4–5 times the 8px base unit). Centered within it is a single, all-caps message, horizontally centered and vertically aligned in the middle of the bar. Directly beneath, a charcoal navigation/header bar runs full width, about 64–72px tall, containing a centered max-width content band (~1100–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid. Within this band, the header is divided visually into three horizontal segments: left nav (approx. 30% width), center logo (40%), and right utility area (30%). Below the header, a full-bleed hero image stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, with a height of roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop. The hero image’s content is horizontally centered, but a semi-transparent overlay box or simply left-aligned text is positioned in the lower-left quadrant of the hero: a 'Contact Support' heading and a single-line description, aligned to the same content max-width as the header, so the left edge of the hero text lines up vertically with content below. Below the hero, the main page content is housed in a centered container (same 1100–1200px width) with 48–64px of top margin from the hero. Inside this container, the main support area and the FAQ block stack vertically. Vertical spacing between main blocks follows the 32–48px rhythm: 48px between hero and main contact section, 64px between the contact section and the FAQ block, and about 48px of padding at the bottom of the page before any footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary structural scaffold, full-width and static (not sticky), with the interior content bands centered. All inner components scroll within this shell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the charcoal navigation/header bar, the cart summary indicator lives in the rightmost utility segment. It is placed at the far right edge of the max-width content band, aligning with the rightmost grid column. The component appears as a compact icon-button (e.g., a cart icon) with a small numeric badge indicating item count. It is horizontally aligned with the wishlist summary to its immediate left and vertically centered within the header bar. There is approximately 16–24px of horizontal padding between this cart indicator and the wishlist indicator; 12–16px internal padding around the icon ensures it has a clear tap/click area. On wider screens, the cart summary occupies roughly 5–8% of the header’s inner width. On smaller screens, it remains pinned to the top-right corner of the header, staying visible but still inside the header height, and may shrink slightly in padding while keeping the badge clearly legible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the header; it scrolls off-screen with the header when the user moves down the page (the header is not sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist summary indicator is placed in the same right utility segment of the header, directly to the left of the cart summary. It appears as a smaller icon-button (e.g., a heart icon) optionally with a subtle numeric badge if there are saved items. The wishlist icon and the cart icon form a compact horizontal cluster at the right side of the header, with roughly 16px of spacing between them. Both are vertically centered within the header’s height and aligned to the top baseline of the logo in the center segment. The wishlist indicator aligns to the same grid columns as the cart; for example, it may sit over the second-to-last column while the cart sits over the last column. Internal padding of 12–16px ensures adequate clickable area while preserving the minimal, modern feel. On narrow viewports, the wishlist remains to the left of the cart, and both scale down proportionally while maintaining ample spacing so they don’t visually merge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header; scrolls out of view with the rest of the header as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support info panel is the left-hand column of the main contact section beneath the hero. It sits inside the centered content container, aligned to the left edge of the grid, and occupies about 35–40% of the container width on desktop. Visually, it appears as a tall, solid red block with a slightly inset, card-like feel. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the contact form on the right, creating a clean, unified horizontal band. The panel has generous internal padding—approximately 32px on all sides—allowing for a large heading like 'Need help?' plus a concise set of support details (e.g., email, phone, response times, links to status pages). Content inside is left-aligned, with vertical spacing of 16–24px between lines or groups. The panel’s height roughly matches or is slightly shorter than the contact form on the right; if the form is taller due to many fields, the red block may end above the bottom edge of the form, reinforcing that it is a compact, high-impact summary. On larger screens, its left and right edges align with the underlying grid columns, keeping the red block visually anchored and not floating. On tablets and mobile devices, this panel becomes a full-width block stacked above the contact form, maintaining its red background and 24–32px padding but reducing vertical margins to fit comfortably within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element; it participates in a responsive two-column layout on desktop and collapses to a stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form sits directly to the right of the support info panel, forming the right column of the main contact section. It occupies approximately 60–65% of the content container width on desktop and is aligned so that its top edge is level with the top of the red support panel. The form is presented as a white (or very light) card against the page background, with a subtle border or shadow to differentiate it. The left edge of the form card sits about 24px to the right of the support panel, creating a deliberate, breathable gutter between the two columns that follows the 3x base unit (24px). Inside the form, a clear heading such as 'Send us a message' appears at the top, followed by a vertical stack of fields (name, email, subject, message, etc.) with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. Side padding of 32px and top/bottom padding of 32–40px maintain the spacious editorial feel. The primary submit button appears aligned to the left within the form content, near the bottom, with 24px of top margin from the last form field. On desktop, the form’s height is likely slightly greater than the support panel, extending further down to indicate the depth of interaction available. On tablet and mobile, this card expands to 100% of the width, stacking under the red support panel. Top margin between the two blocks on mobile is around 24–32px, preserving separation while maintaining a cohesive section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the main content; adapts from a right-hand column to a full-width stacked card on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback banner appears conditionally within the main contact section, anchored to the top of the right column above the contact form card. When visible (e.g., after the user submits the form), it spans the full width of the contact-form column, aligning its left and right edges exactly with the form’s card edges. The banner stands about 40–56px tall, depending on content, with 16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, and uses a slightly tinted background color (e.g., soft green for success, warm amber for warnings) while keeping typography simple and in line with the site’s style. It sits directly above the form, with a small 8–16px gap separating its bottom edge from the form card’s top edge, creating a compact visual group. The message text is left-aligned, and an optional small icon can appear to the left of the text. On narrow screens where the form becomes full width, the banner also becomes full width and stacks above the form, maintaining the same margins and padding. When not present, the contact form moves up to occupy its space, so the layout does not leave a gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the document flow above the contact form; not fixed or floating. It pushes other content down when shown and collapses completely when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ link block forms the final major section of the page, positioned directly beneath the main two-column contact section. It begins about 64px below the bottom edge of the contact section, maintaining the generous vertical rhythm observed in the mockup. The block spans the full page width but constrains its content to the same centered max-width container used elsewhere. Visually, it appears as a horizontal band with a very light background tint (e.g., off-white or very pale gray) to distinguish it from the main content background. Inside, content is structured in a two-part layout: on the left (about 40% of the width), a brief heading such as 'Browse Help & FAQs' and a one- or two-line description; on the right (about 60%), a small grid or vertical list of 3–5 prominent links to key help topics (e.g., 'Account & Billing', 'Technical Issues', 'Order Status'), each occupying a row with 12–16px vertical spacing. On wide screens, these links can be arranged in two columns inside the right area if content allows, but the overall density stays light. Padding inside the band is approximately 32–40px top and bottom, and 24–32px on the sides, yielding an airy, editorial feel. On tablets and mobile, the heading and description stack above the link group, with center- or left-aligned text and comfortable vertical spacing so that the section feels like a clear, secondary option after the primary contact tools.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the main content, expanding to full width on small screens while keeping its inner content centered. It scrolls naturally with the page.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: This page loads and displays the site's Privacy Policy, including how personal data is collected, stored, used, and shared, and explains user data rights. It also shows the current cart and wishlist status in the header via summary interfaces.

core_features: Retrieve and display the latest Privacy Policy content using getLegalPageContent with the appropriate pageType for this page.
Show the Privacy Policy title and last-updated timestamp from the legal content service.
Render the full legal body text of the Privacy Policy without hardcoded legal copy, relying entirely on the backend source.
Provide in-page navigation to scroll to specific sections of the Privacy Policy based on URL parameters (e.g., a selected section) and/or anchors within the content.
Allow external deep links to open the Privacy Policy at a specific subsection using URL parameters.
Display a cart summary indicator in the header using getCartSummary (item count, pricing summary, preorder indicator).
Display a wishlist summary indicator in the header using getWishlistSummary (item count).
Offer basic utility actions that do not require additional interfaces, such as printing the policy via browser print functionality.
Handle loading and error states for the legal content, cart summary, and wishlist summary without falling back to hardcoded legal or business data.
user_workflows:
1) View the Privacy Policy from the footer:
   1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer, which navigates to privacy.html (optionally with query parameters).
   2. On page initialization, the page-shell component triggers getLegalPageContent with the pageType associated to the Privacy Policy page.
   3. The legal-content component receives { title, body, lastUpdated } and updates the document/page title and main heading.
   4. The legal body is rendered into the content area.
   5. In parallel, the header components call getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to show the current item counts and totals.
   6. User scrolls to read the policy, or uses any available in-page navigation to jump between sections.

2) Open a specific policy section via direct link:
   1. User clicks or receives a direct link such as privacy.html?section=<sectionKey>, where sectionKey identifies a logical section in the policy.
   2. On load, the page parses the section URL parameter and stores the target sectionKey in page state.
   3. The page triggers getLegalPageContent; once body is loaded, the section-navigation component analyzes the rendered content (for example, by locating heading anchors or named sections) and attempts to find the section that matches sectionKey.
   4. If a matching section anchor is found, the page programmatically scrolls that section into view and updates the URL hash or keeps the query parameter as-is.
   5. If no matching section is found, the page remains at the top of the document while still displaying the full policy.

3) Navigate between sections using in-page navigation:
   1. After getLegalPageContent resolves and the Privacy Policy body is rendered, the TOC component extracts a list of sections (e.g., from headings or metadata embedded in the body string).
   2. The TOC component displays a list of clickable section entries.
   3. When the user clicks a section entry, the component updates the URL parameter section=<sectionKey> using history APIs and scrolls to the corresponding section.
   4. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons and the section parameter changes, the component listens for URL changes and updates the visible highlighted section and scroll position accordingly.

4) From Privacy Policy to cart or wishlist:
   1. While reading the policy, the user can see cart and wishlist indicators in the header populated via getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary.
   2. The user clicks the cart icon; the cart-summary component handles the click and navigates to cart.html (URL-only navigation, no additional APIs from this page).
   3. Similarly, clicking the wishlist icon navigates to wishlist.html.
   4. When the user navigates back to privacy.html (via browser or links), the page re-runs getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary to restore the current state based solely on the interfaces.

5) Print or save the Privacy Policy:
   1. The user clicks a 'Print' or 'Open print dialog' control in the utility actions area.
   2. The utility component triggers the browser's native print dialog (no backend call required).
   3. The user confirms printing or saving to PDF from the browser dialog; the page content printed is whatever was dynamically loaded via getLegalPageContent.
interactions: Click on footer 'Privacy Policy' link: navigates to privacy.html (with optional section and other query parameters).
Page load (onInit) on privacy.html: triggers getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary.
Async response from getLegalPageContent: updates the main heading, last-updated label, and renders the policy body content.
Async response from getCartSummary: updates the cart icon badge with itemCount and may show a condensed price summary tooltip or label.
Async response from getWishlistSummary: updates the wishlist icon badge with itemCount.
Click on cart icon: navigates to cart.html.
Click on wishlist icon: navigates to wishlist.html.
Change in URL query parameter section (via link click or browser navigation): triggers re-evaluation of which section is active and scrolls the relevant section into view.
Click on an entry in the in-page table of contents: updates the URL parameter section to the selected section key and scrolls the corresponding section into view.
Click on print action: triggers the browser's print dialog for the current page.
Error in getLegalPageContent: legal-content component displays a generic error state and offers a retry action that re-calls getLegalPageContent without using any hardcoded policy text.
Error in getCartSummary or getWishlistSummary: header components show a generic fallback state (for example, no counts) until a retry is triggered or the user refreshes the page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Privacy Policy page uses URL parameters and hash fragments to control which content is emphasized and how the user navigates within the policy. The base page is privacy.html. Optional query parameters include:
- section: privacy.html?section=<sectionKey>
  - sectionKey is a logical identifier used by the page to correlate to a particular section of the dynamically loaded legal body. It is not hardcoded in the page; instead, the client extracts or maps section identifiers based on data or structure available in the body returned by getLegalPageContent.
  - On initial page load, the page parses the section parameter and stores it in an in-memory state variable (e.g., currentSectionKey).
  - After the body from getLegalPageContent is rendered, the section-navigation component attempts to locate the corresponding section in the document (for example, by matching to section IDs or data attributes within the body) and scrolls it into view.
  - When the user selects a section from the in-page navigation, the page updates the section query parameter via the browser history API (e.g., using pushState or replaceState) without reloading the entire page. The in-memory state currentSectionKey is updated accordingly.

- Additional URL parameters can be added by other parts of the application (for example, a language or source parameter) but are not interpreted by this page's specific logic unless explicitly defined by the broader application. This specification only uses the section parameter for functional behavior.

The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage for state. On every page load or navigation back to privacy.html, the current view is reconstructed solely from the URL parameters and the responses from getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary. All user-visible content and timestamps are read from the interfaces; no legal content, timestamps, or cart values are hardcoded in the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the Privacy Policy page, orchestrates calls to getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary on load, and manages routing of data to child components based on URL parameters.
Displays global navigation and hosts the cart and wishlist summary indicators, keeping them synchronized with getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary.
Calls getCartSummary (via the page shell or header-bar), displays the current itemCount and pricing summary in the header, and navigates to the cart page when clicked.
Calls getWishlistSummary (via the page shell or header-bar), displays the current wishlist itemCount in the header, and navigates to the wishlist page when clicked.

Requests the Privacy Policy content using getLegalPageContent, then renders the returned title, lastUpdated timestamp, and body. It exposes hooks for the TOC and section-navigation components to work with the fully rendered content.

Builds a table of contents based on the headings or metadata found in the loaded Privacy Policy body. Allows users to click section entries, which updates the URL section parameter and instructs the section-navigation component to scroll to the corresponding section.

Listens for changes in the section URL parameter and, after the legal body is rendered, scrolls the corresponding section into view if it can be matched. Maintains the currently active section state in memory based solely on the URL and rendered content structure.

Displays metadata about the Privacy Policy such as the lastUpdated timestamp returned by getLegalPageContent. It does not hardcode any dates or policy version strings, relying entirely on the API response.

Provides non-backend actions related to the Privacy Policy, such as opening the browser print dialog for the current page. It does not introduce any additional external interfaces or hardcoded legal content.
Shows a generic error state for the legal content area if getLegalPageContent fails, and offers a retry action that re-invokes getLegalPageContent without using any static or hardcoded policy text.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top-to-bottom structure: a slim announcement bar and global navigation combined as the header at the very top, followed by a full-width policy metadata band that acts as the hero equivalent for this legal page. Below that, the main content area uses a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a narrower left column for the Privacy Policy TOC / in‑page navigation, and a wider right column for the policy body. Utility actions sit near the top of the right column, and a subtle section scroller hugs the right edge of the content column. An error state, when present, occupies the main content area where the policy text would normally load.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top-to-bottom structure: a slim announcement bar and global navigation combined as the header at the very top, followed by a full-width policy metadata band that acts as the hero equivalent for this legal page. Below that, the main content area uses a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a narrower left column for the Privacy Policy TOC / in‑page navigation, and a wider right column for the policy body. Utility actions sit near the top of the right column, and a subtle section scroller hugs the right edge of the content column. An error state, when present, occupies the main content area where the policy text would normally load.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header (announcement + nav) occupies roughly the top 12–15% of the viewport height, with the policy metadata bar taking another 12–18% as a strong, full-width band beneath it. The remainder of the viewport is devoted to the two-column policy content area. Horizontally, the main content container is centered with a max width around 1100–1200px, sitting on a full-width dark background. Inside this container, the TOC column uses about 30–35% of the width, while the policy text column uses 65–70%, echoing the slightly asymmetric split from the featured product section in the mockup. Vertical spacing between major bands (header → metadata → content) is in the 32–48px range to keep an airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and deliberate. The header elements are separated with sufficient padding and gaps to avoid clutter on a dark background. The metadata bar uses large typography for the page title and roomy vertical padding to feel like a calm, editorial intro rather than a cramped system banner. The main policy text uses a readable line length and generous line height, with extra spacing before section headings. The TOC column is compact but not dense; items have 8–12px vertical separation and clear hierarchy, matching the curated, minimal aesthetic seen in the mockup’s featured section.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first caught by the dark announcement strip at the very top, then settles on the centered logo in the navigation bar. From there it glides rightward to the search, wishlist, and cart indicators. As the user looks down, the bright, lighter policy metadata band forms the next strong focal block—the page title and last-updated details act like the hero text. From that band, the gaze is pulled down-left to the solid, structured TOC column (functionally echoing the bold red left panel in the mockup), then across to the right where the main policy content begins and continues downward. The section scroller on the far right appears later in the flow as a subtle helper, reinforcing a Z-like pattern from header to metadata to TOC to content.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page sits within a dark, modern e‑commerce frame that mirrors the bookstore mockup but swaps the immersive hero photograph for a calm, editorial legal intro. A slim black announcement strip runs across the very top, followed by a charcoal navigation bar where left-aligned links, a centered logo, and right-aligned search, wishlist, and cart create a balanced header. Directly beneath, a full-width, softly lit metadata band introduces the Privacy Policy title, last updated date, and brief summary, spanning the entire page width but visually anchored to a centered content column. Below this band, the layout resolves into a two-column grid: on the left, a structured TOC panel runs vertically, guiding users between policy sections; on the right, the main legal content flows in a wide, airy column with large headings and comfortable line spacing. Utility action buttons cluster near the top-right of this content column, while a slender section scroller hovers along its right edge, staying visible as the user reads. If an error occurs loading the policy, a prominent but clean error panel takes over the main content column without disrupting the overall structure. The net effect is a legal page that feels as considered and curated as the storefront: dark chrome framing, clear hierarchy, and a strong left-right rhythm that leads readers naturally through the policy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-page-shell is the overarching canvas that establishes the centered, editorial layout within a dark e-commerce frame. It spans the full browser width and height with a charcoal or very dark neutral background, against which the main content container sits. The shell defines a max-width central column of approximately 1100–1200px, horizontally centered in the viewport, and applies the global vertical rhythm: about 32–48px of vertical separation between major sections (header, metadata bar, main content). Inside this shell, all interior components (policy-metadata-bar, privacy-toc, legal-content-loader, section-navigation, privacy-utility-actions, legal-error-state) are aligned to the same underlying 12-column grid. On large screens, the shell ensures that the main content block (TOC + policy text) is vertically positioned so that its top edge starts roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the metadata bar, preserving the spacious feel. On smaller screens, the shell collapses the two-column layout into a single column with the TOC stacking above the policy text, while maintaining consistent side padding (16–24px) from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page background container that constrains interior components to a centered max-width grid; controls responsive behavior (two-column on desktop, single-column stack on mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-bar sits at the absolute top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width. It is composed of two stacked horizontal bands: a slim announcement strip on top and a primary navigation row beneath. The announcement strip is a flat black bar approximately 40px tall, with a single line of centered, all-caps white text running across its full width. Directly below, the main navigation bar is a charcoal band about 72–88px tall. Inside this bar, the content aligns to the same centered max-width container as the rest of the page. The nav contents are divided into three horizontal segments: the left third holds primary links such as HOME and CATALOG; the middle 40% is reserved for a centered logo, vertically aligned within the bar; and the right third contains the search control followed by the wishlist and cart indicators arranged in a horizontal row. Each segment has horizontal padding of 24–32px from its edges, and links have 16–24px of spacing between them, giving an open, modern feel. The header-bar visually caps the page and slightly overlaps the top of the privacy-metadata-bar in the user’s field of view, reinforcing the stacked, full-width hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport after scrolling; remains fixed while the rest of the page scrolls beneath it, ensuring persistent access to navigation, cart, and wishlist.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-indicator sits inside the rightmost third of the header-bar, aligned horizontally to the far right edge of the centered content container. It appears as a compact icon-plus-count element—typically a shopping bag or cart icon with a small circular badge at its top-right corner showing the item count. The indicator is positioned to the right of the wishlist-summary-indicator, separated from it by about 16–24px of horizontal space. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the navigation bar. The icon is clear but restrained, matching the minimal aesthetic, and sized so that together with its badge it occupies roughly 32–40px in width. It does not extend beyond the nav bar height and leaves at least 16px of padding from the right edge of the content container. On narrower screens, it remains within the right-hand controls cluster, stacking or aligning below the search as space requires, but always visible without overlapping other header elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the header-bar’s right cluster; moves with the header as it sticks to the top during scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-summary-indicator is placed directly to the left of the cart-summary-indicator in the header-bar’s right-hand utility cluster.
