It presents as a heart or bookmark icon with a small numeric badge showing the count of wishlist items, visually sized and styled to match the cart indicator. It is horizontally aligned with the cart and search controls, with 16–24px of horizontal space separating it from both neighbors. Like the cart indicator, it is vertically centered within the nav bar’s height, leaving 12–16px of padding above and below to maintain the light, uncluttered feel. Both wishlist and cart indicators sit on the charcoal background, using white or light icons with subtle red accents in the badges to tie into the brand’s accent color. On small screens, the wishlist indicator remains grouped with the cart, possibly forming a compact row beneath the search icon while still respecting 8–12px of spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the header-bar; follows the header’s sticky behavior and remains visible at the top of the viewport during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-metadata-bar sits directly beneath the header-bar, stretching the full browser width as a distinct horizontal band that functions like a subdued hero. It has a lighter background than the surrounding dark shell—either white or a very light neutral—creating strong contrast with the header above. Vertically, it occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop, with 24–32px of padding on the top and bottom. Inside, content is confined to the centered max-width container. The left two-thirds of this band contains the primary metadata: a large, bold page title (e.g., ‘Privacy Policy’) that spans about half the container width, followed by a short descriptive sentence or tagline and a smaller line indicating the last updated date. These elements are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing, aligned to the left. The right third of the bar is reserved for higher-level utilities or summary info (e.g., a brief status note or shortcut link), and also serves as the horizontal anchor for the privacy-utility-actions which may visually overlap into the top of the main content area below. The bottom edge of the metadata bar aligns visually with the top of the two-column content grid below, maintaining the stacked, editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band; scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page, but its combined height and padding ensure it dominates the initial view beneath the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-toc occupies the left column of the main content area directly beneath the policy-metadata-bar. Inside the centered max-width container, this TOC column spans approximately 30–35% of the available width, leaving the remaining 65–70% for the main policy content on the right. The TOC begins about 32–40px below the bottom edge of the metadata bar, aligning its top with the top of the legal-content-loader on the right, creating a clean horizontal baseline. It is rendered on a subtle panel—either a slightly lighter or darker neutral than the page background—with 16–24px of internal padding and rounded corners to echo the solid red block from the mockup without using such an intense color. Section titles are listed vertically with 8–12px spacing between items and 16–24px above each top-level section group, allowing the list to feel structured yet airy. Active or hovered items use a red accent line or bullet on the left to mirror the brand’s accent color. On larger screens, the TOC panel is sticky within its column: as the user scrolls, it stays pinned roughly 80px below the sticky header, ensuring the list of sections remains visible while the right-hand content scrolls. At mobile widths, the TOC collapses into a horizontal chip or dropdown bar sitting above the main content, spanning close to 100% of the viewport width with maintained 16px side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the left column on desktop (remaining visible as long as there is content to scroll), with an offset from the top to avoid overlapping the sticky header; transforms into a non-sticky stacked element above content on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-content-loader occupies the main right column of the two-column layout, starting directly to the right of the privacy-toc and aligned with its top. Within the central container, this column spans approximately 65–70% of the width. The loader’s content area starts 32–40px below the metadata bar to maintain consistent vertical rhythm. When loading or displaying content, it uses a clear hierarchy: a large, left-aligned policy heading (duplicating or complementing the main title) at the top, followed by the body text structured into sections and subsections. Paragraphs are set at a comfortable line length and line height, with 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and 24–32px before section headings. The content sits on a clean, light background (usually the same as the metadata bar’s base color), with generous left and right padding of 24px inside the column boundaries, and at least 32px of separation from the TOC panel. If loading states are used, skeleton lines or a subtle spinner are centered at the top of this column, never overlapping the TOC. When content fails to load, the legal-error-state replaces the body of this column entirely. On smaller screens, this loader becomes full-width beneath the TOC, maintaining the same vertical spacing rules but using smaller side paddings (16–20px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable page content; its height grows with content length and determines the overall scroll depth. Acts as the anchor region for the section-navigation positioning on its right edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation appears as a slim, vertically oriented control aligned with the right edge of the legal-content-loader’s column. On desktop, it is positioned so that its left edge sits flush or slightly inset from the outer boundary of the main content column, approximately 16–24px from the text block, visually hovering over the light background. Vertically, it is centered relative to the visible viewport, typically starting a little below the top of the policy text once the user has scrolled past the initial metadata. The navigation comprises small dots, numbers, or short labels stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing, each representing a major section in the policy. Active section indicators use a red accent or increased size to stand out, but the overall component remains visually subtle so it does not compete with the main text. On large screens, this control is sticky within the viewport: it remains fixed in place along the right edge as the user scrolls through the policy sections. On tablets and smaller screens, it either collapses into a compact horizontal scroller beneath the TOC and above the main text or hides entirely, letting the TOC handle in-page navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky vertical control along the right edge of the main content column on desktop, following scroll but maintaining a fixed position relative to the viewport; adaptive or hidden on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-utility-actions component presents as a compact horizontal action bar positioned near the top-right of the main content area, bridging the metadata bar and the legal-content-loader. Within the centered max-width container, it is right-aligned above the main body text: roughly aligned with the right boundary of the content column, and typically placed 16–24px below the last line of metadata text and 16–24px above the opening of the policy content. It consists of 2–4 pill-shaped buttons (e.g., ‘Download PDF’, ‘Print’, ‘Contact Us’) laid out in a row with 8–16px of horizontal spacing between them. Each button has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, following the spacing system. The buttons use minimal styling—white or light fills with thin borders and red text accents or, for primary actions, a red fill with white text—so they feel functional but not overpowering. On desktop, this bar slightly overlaps the conceptual boundary between the metadata band and the content area, reinforcing the connection between policy information and actions. On mobile, the actions move to a full-width row directly below the metadata bar and above the TOC or content, with buttons wrapping to a second line if needed while preserving at least 8px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the policy but remains in a predictable position near the top of the content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-error-state is an inline, attention-grabbing panel that appears within the right-hand content column wherever the legal-content-loader would normally render text. When triggered (e.g., if the policy fails to load), it occupies the full width of the legal-content-loader’s column, starting at the same top alignment point beneath the metadata bar and adjacent to the TOC. The panel uses a light background with a strong red accent bar or icon at the top-left corner to signal error, but stays consistent with the site’s minimal style: rounded corners, 16–24px internal padding, and generous white space. The error title is bold and slightly larger than body text; explanatory copy and any suggested actions (like a retry button) are stacked with 8–16px spacing. The TOC remains visible on the left; the error panel does not overlay or hide it, but visually dominates the right column so users immediately understand content failed to load. On smaller screens, the error panel expands to full width below the TOC, still respecting side paddings. When the error state is not active, this component remains hidden and does not consume space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered inline; replaces the legal-content-loader’s body region without altering the overall layout structure. Scrolls naturally with the rest of the content when visible.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the legal terms governing use of the ebook store and purchases, with live cart and wishlist summaries.

core_features: Load and display the Terms & Conditions content via getLegalPageContent with the appropriate pageType for this page.
Show lightweight cart and wishlist summaries in the header using getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary so users can see current item counts and totals while reading.
Provide in-page navigation to sections of the Terms & Conditions text based on URL parameters (e.g., scroll/highlight a section when a query parameter is present).
Enable navigation from Terms & Conditions to the Privacy Policy (privacy.html) and Refunds & Digital Goods Policy (refunds.html) via links contained in the legal content or supplemental navigation.
React to URL changes (e.g., section changes via query parameters) without reloading the entire page content, keeping legal text and summaries in sync.
user_workflows: View Terms & Conditions from footer: User clicks the footer link to terms.html → Page initializes and calls getLegalPageContent with a Terms-specific pageType → When the response returns, the legal content viewer renders title, body, and lastUpdated → In parallel, header components call getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to show the current cart total and wishlist count.

Jump to a specific Terms section via URL: User follows a link to terms.html with a query parameter indicating a section (e.g., terms.html?section=some-section-key) → On load, the page reads the section parameter → After getLegalPageContent finishes and the body is rendered, the component scrolls to the target section (if found) and visually indicates it as active.

Navigate from Terms to Privacy Policy: While reading, the user clicks a link in the Terms text or in a supplemental list that points to privacy.html → The click triggers a normal navigation to privacy.html, preserving any relevant query parameters if needed (e.g., ref=terms).

Navigate from Terms to Refunds & Digital Goods Policy: User locates the clause referring to refunds and clicks the link that targets refunds.html → The browser navigates to refunds.html so the user can see more detailed refund terms.

Check cart status while on Terms: User looks at the header cart icon while on terms.html → The header has already called getCartSummary and displays the current item count and total → If the user navigates away or refreshes, the summary is refreshed using getCartSummary again.

Check wishlist status while on Terms: User glances at the wishlist icon while reading → The header uses getWishlistSummary to show how many items are on the wishlist → This can be re-fetched on page load or when the page regains focus to keep the count up to date.
interactions: Page load on terms.html: On initial load, a lifecycle event triggers getLegalPageContent with a pageType value designated for Terms & Conditions, then binds the returned title, body, and lastUpdated to the legal content components.

Header cart summary: On page load (and optionally on visibility changes), a lifecycle event triggers getCartSummary with no parameters; the returned itemCount, subtotal, discountTotal, total, and hasPreorders are bound to the cart summary icon and tooltip or popover.
Header wishlist summary: On page load (and optionally on visibility changes), a lifecycle event triggers getWishlistSummary; the returned itemCount is bound to the wishlist icon badge.

In-page section navigation: When a user clicks a section link (e.g., an internal anchor or a generated table-of-contents link) within the Terms page, the component updates the URL query parameter (section=...) using the History API and scrolls to the corresponding anchor in the rendered legal body.

URL-driven section highlighting: When the URL query parameter section changes (either on initial load or via browser navigation buttons), a handler reads the section parameter and adjusts the scroll position and highlight state of the corresponding section within the Terms body.

Click to Privacy Policy: When the user clicks a link in the Terms content that points to privacy.html, the browser navigates to privacy.html. If the link is enhanced by the application, a click handler can optionally append a ref=terms parameter to the target URL.

Click to Refunds Policy: When the user clicks a link in the Terms content that points to refunds.html, the browser navigates to refunds.html, optionally adding ref=terms or section parameters if defined by business rules.

Refresh of summaries: If the page listens to focus or visibility change events, it can re-trigger getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary when the user returns to the Terms page from another tab or after interacting with cart/wishlist on another page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is represented in the URL for terms.html using query parameters. A query parameter named section controls which part of the Terms body is considered active; when present, the page waits until getLegalPageContent has resolved and the body is rendered, then scrolls to an anchor or marker that matches the section parameter and applies an active style. If the section parameter is absent, the page displays the content from the top. Another optional parameter, ref, can be used to record where the user came from (for example, ref=checkout or ref=footer) and to adjust contextual UI elements such as a 'Back' link; the logic reads ref on load and stores it in in-memory state, without persisting it outside the current page. Navigation from this page to privacy.html or refunds.html can preserve or modify ref in the outgoing URLs according to business rules. Cart and wishlist summaries do not depend on URL parameters; they are always requested fresh when the page is loaded or when configured lifecycle events fire. All state that affects rendering is derived either from the current URL parameters or from the latest responses of getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary, without using localStorage.

components.functionality: Fetches the Terms & Conditions content for this page and exposes it to child components once loaded.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when terms.html is loaded; determines the correct pageType value for Terms & Conditions (for example, based on route configuration) and calls getLegalPageContent with that pageType. On success, stores the returned object in component state and notifies dependent components that the legal content is ready.
onReloadRequested: Optional handler that can be invoked (e.g., via a developer tool or internal action) to refetch getLegalPageContent if needed and update the displayed content.
onError: Handles failures from getLegalPageContent by updating an error state that downstream components can render as an error message or fallback, without providing any static fallback content.

components.functionality: Renders the Terms & Conditions title, last-updated metadata, and body text, and supports in-page navigation and highlighting of specific sections.
event_handlers: onContentReady: Triggered when LegalPageContent.body is bound and inserted into the DOM; reads URL.query.section and, if present, scrolls to the matching section anchor and applies an active state to that section.

onSectionLinkClick: When the user clicks an internal section link within the Terms content (such as a table-of-contents entry or anchor link), prevents default behavior if necessary, updates URL.query.section via the History API, and scrolls to the corresponding section.
onUrlChange: Listens for browser navigation events (such as back/forward) that modify URL.query.section and updates the highlighted section and scroll position accordingly.
onErrorStateChange: If the upstream legal-page-loader reports an error, responds by displaying a non-specific error indication without embedding hardcoded Terms text.

components.functionality: Displays a compact cart summary (item count and pricing) in the page header while the user views the Terms & Conditions.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the header is rendered on terms.html; triggers getCartSummary with no parameters and binds the response to the cart icon badge and any associated tooltip or popover.
onVisibilityChange: Optional; when the document visibility changes back to visible, can re-call getCartSummary to ensure data is up to date.
onCartIconClick: Navigates the user to the cart page (e.g., cart.html) using standard navigation, without altering or caching cart details locally.
onApiError: Handles failures from getCartSummary by clearing or hiding the summary values and optionally showing a generic error indicator.

components.functionality: Shows the total number of items in the user’s wishlist as a badge on the wishlist icon while on the Terms & Conditions page.
event_handlers: onInit: Invoked when the header loads; triggers getWishlistSummary and binds the returned itemCount to the wishlist icon badge.
onVisibilityChange: Optional; may re-invoke getWishlistSummary when the page regains focus so the count reflects recent changes made elsewhere.
onWishlistIconClick: Navigates the user to the wishlist page (e.g., wishlist.html) using standard navigation.
onApiError: Handles failures from getWishlistSummary by clearing or hiding the badge and optionally showing a generic error indicator.

components.functionality: Enhances and manages links within the Terms & Conditions content that point to other legal pages such as the Privacy Policy and Refunds & Digital Goods Policy.
event_handlers: onContentScan: After LegalPageContent.body is rendered, scans rendered links for targets matching privacy.html and refunds.html and attaches enhanced click handlers to them if needed.

onPrivacyLinkClick: When a link to privacy.html is clicked, optionally appends a ref parameter derived from URL.query.ref or a default value indicating that navigation originated from Terms, then allows navigation to proceed.
onRefundsLinkClick: When a link to refunds.html is clicked, optionally appends a ref parameter indicating origin from Terms and allows navigation to proceed.
onExternalLegalLinkClick: For any other external or legal-related links within the body, ensures standard navigation behavior without overriding the target.

components.functionality: Centralizes read/write access to URL query parameters that affect the Terms & Conditions page, such as section and ref, and exposes them to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the current query parameters from the URL and exposes section and ref values to subscribing components like legal-content-viewer and legal-inline-navigation.
onSectionChangeRequest: Called by components (e.g., legal-content-viewer) when a new section should become active; updates URL.query.section using the History API without reloading the page.
onPopState: Listens for browser back/forward events and updates subscribed components when query parameters (especially section) change.
onRefUpdateRequest: If the originating context changes (for example, navigation into Terms from different sources), can update URL.query.ref accordingly, again without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the stacked, full-width structure from the mockup: a slim announcement bar at the very top, then the main navigation/header with three segments (left nav, center logo, right utilities including cart and wishlist summaries). Directly beneath is a full-bleed, atmospheric bookstore hero that introduces the 'Terms & Conditions' title. Below the hero, the legal content section uses a two-column layout reminiscent of the featured product bands from the mockup: a narrower left column for the inline legal navigation (taking the role of the bold red panel) and a wider right column for the main terms text. Invisible system components (loader and URL state manager) sit logically within this hierarchy, affecting behavior but not the visual structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the stacked, full-width structure from the mockup: a slim announcement bar at the very top, then the main navigation/header with three segments (left nav, center logo, right utilities including cart and wishlist summaries). Directly beneath is a full-bleed, atmospheric bookstore hero that introduces the 'Terms & Conditions' title. Below the hero, the legal content section uses a two-column layout reminiscent of the featured product bands from the mockup: a narrower left column for the inline legal navigation (taking the role of the bold red panel) and a wider right column for the main terms text. Invisible system components (loader and URL state manager) sit logically within this hierarchy, affecting behavior but not the visual structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The announcement bar and header together occupy roughly the top 12–15% of the viewport, consistent with the mockup. The hero banner with the bookstore photography occupies about 30–40% of the initial viewport height, enough to feel immersive but slightly shorter than a marketing hero since this is a legal page. The remaining space is dedicated to the legal content area. Horizontally, the main content container is centered with a max width of about 1120px, using a two-column layout where the left inline navigation column takes ~30–35% of the content width and the right legal text column takes ~65–70%. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero to content area, content blocks) follows the 32–48px rhythm to keep an editorial, breathable feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and hero remain relatively light and airy, with comfortable padding around nav links and utilities. In the legal content area, the density increases slightly due to long-form text, but is controlled with generous line spacing, ample margins, and clear section breaks. The inline navigation on the left presents a concise list of section links without crowding, using a solid color block and large section labels. The terms text on the right is structured into clearly separated sections (using the spacing scale) so the page reads as a curated, editorial legal document rather than dense, cramped terms.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters at the announcement bar and then drops to the centered logo in the header, branching to the left nav links and right-hand utilities (search, cart, wishlist summaries). From there, the gaze moves down into the atmospheric hero image, where a centered 'Terms & Conditions' heading anchors attention. As the user scrolls, the strong color block on the left (inline navigation) becomes the next focal point, pulling the eye down-left before it moves across to the right into the large, readable legal text. This preserves a Z-like flow: top bar → center logo → right utilities → down into hero → down-left to the colored navigation panel → across to the main terms content.

layout.overall_description: The Terms & Conditions page sits within the same dark, image-driven frame as the main storefront. A thin black announcement bar stretches across the very top, followed by a charcoal navigation header whose content is centered in a max-width band, with links on the left, the logo in the middle, and search plus cart and wishlist summaries on the right. Immediately below, a full-bleed hero photograph of a warm, atmospheric bookstore fills roughly a third of the screen, subtly darkened so a large, white 'Terms & Conditions' title can sit centered or slightly left of center without competing with the image. As you scroll past the hero, the main content container appears: a wide, centered band with a two-column layout. On the left, a solid color panel—using the brand’s rich red or deep accent tone—runs down the page, housing an inline navigation of key legal sections in a vertical list. On the right, against a clean white or very light background, the legal text flows in a single column with generous margins and line height, broken into clearly spaced sections that echo the editorial feel of product features rather than a wall of text. A loading state can temporarily overlay or occupy the main content area when terms are being fetched, but otherwise the layout remains calm and stable. Throughout, spacing follows the 8px-based rhythm, and the visual hierarchy guides the user from the global e-commerce context, into the legal hero, and then into a left-to-right reading of the terms aided by the sticky inline navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-page-loader occupies the main content area beneath the hero banner when active. Vertically, it appears starting roughly 32–48px below the bottom edge of the hero image, within the centered max-width content container (around 1100–1200px wide). Horizontally, it is centered within this container, aligned to the same grid as the two-column layout that will hold the inline navigation and legal content once loaded. The loader itself should feel minimal and unobtrusive: for example, a centered spinner or progress indicator with a short status line, occupying roughly the middle third of the viewport height, with at least 32px of whitespace above and below. While the loader is visible, the underlying two-column structure is either not rendered or visually subdued, so the user’s eye is drawn to this single focal point in the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is transient and context-bound: it appears in place of the legal content area (within the main content container below the hero) and disappears once data is fetched. It does not fix itself to the viewport; instead, it scrolls naturally as part of the page, though the typical use will have it visible near the top of the content area during initial page load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-content-viewer forms the primary right-hand column of the main content area beneath the hero. The main content band begins about 32–48px below the hero image and is centered on the page with a max width of approximately 1120px. Within this band, the legal-content-viewer occupies the right 65–70% of the width, starting at the same top baseline as the left inline navigation panel. It presents the Terms & Conditions as a vertically scrolling column of text on a white or very light background, with 24–32px inner padding on all sides to create a comfortable reading frame. The text column itself should be constrained to an optimal reading width (roughly 60–75 characters per line), so in wider viewports there are subtle side margins within this right column. Sections are separated by 24–32px vertical spacing, with section headings set in larger, bold typography that echoes the minimalist title treatment from the mockup’s featured product area. The content-viewer extends down the page as long as the document requires, while maintaining the fixed alignment to the top of the inline navigation panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The legal-content-viewer scrolls normally with the page and remains anchored to the right side of the centered content container. It does not fix or float independently; however, its left edge aligns consistently with the right edge of the inline navigation column, maintaining a stable two-column relationship across the scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-summary sits in the top navigation bar on the right-hand side, sharing the utilities segment with the search control and wishlist summary. The nav bar spans the full width of the viewport with a charcoal background, while its internal content is centered in a max-width band matching the main content container. Within this rightmost 30% segment, the cart summary appears toward the far right edge, after the wishlist summary, with 16–24px horizontal padding separating it from adjacent elements. It presents a compact icon (e.g., cart) with a subtle numeric badge indicating item count; the badge is small but legible, using the brand’s red accent to stand out against the dark background. Vertically, the cart summary is centered within the nav bar’s height, with approximately 12–16px of padding above and below, maintaining the comfortable spacing of other header elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-cart-summary is part of the global, fixed-position or sticky header (depending on site-wide behavior). It remains anchored to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls through the hero and legal content, ensuring persistent access to cart information. It does not overlap the hero or content; instead, the rest of the page scrolls beneath this stable header band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-wishlist-summary is positioned directly to the left of the header-cart-summary within the right-hand utilities segment of the navigation bar. All utilities (search, wishlist, cart) are aligned horizontally in a row, with the wishlist summary occupying a middle position: search is closer to the center of the header, then a small 16–24px gap, then the wishlist summary, followed by a similar gap and then the cart summary at the far right. The wishlist summary uses a compact heart or bookmark icon with a subtle count badge when items exist, styled in a way that mirrors the cart badge but with slightly lower emphasis (e.g., smaller or less saturated) to avoid competing with cart status. Like other header elements, it is vertically centered in the nav bar with consistent 12–16px vertical padding and aligns visually to the same baseline as the nav links on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-wishlist-summary shares the same behavior as the header-cart-summary, living inside the persistent header band that remains at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls. It is not independently sticky; its position is fixed relative to other header utilities, maintaining a stable right-aligned cluster throughout navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-inline-navigation occupies the left-hand column of the main content area beneath the hero banner, echoing the role and visual weight of the red promotional block from the mockup’s featured product section. The main content container begins roughly 32–48px below the hero and is centered on the page. Within this, the inline navigation column takes about 30–35% of the width and runs from the top of the content area downward. The background of this column is a solid brand accent color (ideally the same rich red seen in the mockup), creating a strong vertical block that immediately catches the eye. Inside this block, navigation items (e.g., 'Overview', 'Purchases', 'User Accounts', 'Licensing', 'Liability') are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them and 24–32px padding from the edges. Type is large, high-contrast, and left-aligned, using white or off-white text to stand out against the red. On larger screens, the column’s width is sufficient for short labels to break into one or two lines without feeling cramped. This panel starts at the same top baseline as the legal-content-viewer and extends as far down as needed; where the terms text grows taller, the colored panel may extend below the fold, preserving the two-column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablet viewports, the legal-inline-navigation behaves as a sticky element within the main content area: once the user scrolls past the hero and main content top edge, the navigation panel remains pinned near the top of the viewport while the legal text on the right continues to scroll. This maintains the left-hand focal block and Z-flow guidance. Within its parent content container, it does not overlap the header or hero; on smaller screens, it can gracefully shift to a full-width horizontal block above the legal-content-viewer, but its default behavior is a left-side sticky column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-url-state-manager has no direct visual representation; instead, it governs how the URL reflects and restores the user’s position within the Terms & Conditions. Conceptually, it is tied to both the legal-inline-navigation and the legal-content-viewer: when a user clicks a navigation item on the left or scrolls into a new section on the right, the URL state updates (e.g., with a hash fragment) to reflect the active section. Conversely, loading a URL with a specific section identifier brings the corresponding part of the legal-content-viewer into view and highlights the related item in the inline navigation. This behavior is integrated into the layout so that section transitions feel smooth and aligned with the existing two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The terms-url-state-manager does not occupy any physical space in the layout and does not alter the positioning of other elements. Its effects are purely behavioral, coordinating scroll position and navigation highlighting in response to URL changes. It operates behind the scenes while respecting the fixed header and sticky inline navigation, ensuring that automatic scrolling never pushes these elements off-screen or into overlapping states.

name: Refunds & Digital Goods Policy
title: Refunds & Digital Goods Policy
description: Displays the store’s refund, cancellation, and digital ebook delivery policies, with contextual navigation to help and support, and header access to cart and wishlist summaries.

core_features: Load and display the full Refunds & Digital Goods Policy content via the legal content interface, including title, body, and last-updated metadata.
Provide in-page navigation to specific sections of the policy (e.g., refunds, cancellations, preorders, delivery) based on URL parameters.
Expose contextual links from the policy page to the general help page and to the contact/support page, passing along context such as the current policy section.
Show a lightweight cart summary in the header (item count and totals) using the cart summary interface.
Show a lightweight wishlist summary in the header (item count) using the wishlist summary interface.
Keep URL parameters in sync with the user’s current view (selected policy section) so that deep links to specific sections can be shared or opened from emails.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View overall refund & digital goods policy
1. User opens refunds.html (via footer link or external link).
2. On page load, the page-init logic parses URL parameters (e.g., section).
3. The page calls getLegalPageContent with pageType set for the refund and digital goods policy.
4. The page calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary to populate the header icons.
5. Once getLegalPageContent resolves, the policy title, last-updated date, and body are rendered.
6. The body is parsed into sections based on headings from the returned content.
7. If a section URL parameter is present, the page scrolls to and highlights that section.
8. User reads the policy content, optionally using the table of contents to jump between sections.

Workflow: Deep-link into a specific policy section
1. User opens a URL like refunds.html?section=preorders.
2. The page-init logic reads section=preorders from the URL and stores it as currentSection state.
3. The page calls getLegalPageContent, getCartSummary, and getWishlistSummary in parallel.
4. After the policy body is loaded, the content viewer parses headings into sections and maps a section identifier (such as "preorders") to a specific heading/anchor in the document.
5. The content viewer scrolls to the matching section and visually indicates it as active.
6. The table of contents component marks the same section as active.
7. Contextual help/contact links include the section identifier in their URLs so that the downstream pages know the user’s context.

Workflow: Navigate between policy sections using the table of contents
1. User is on refunds.html and sees a table of contents generated from headings in the loaded policy content.
2. User clicks a table-of-contents entry (for example, the section describing refund time limits).
3. The TOC component determines the associated section identifier for the clicked heading.
4. The TOC component updates the URL query string to set section to this identifier (e.g., refunds.html?section=refund_time_limits) without reloading the page when possible.
5. The currentSection state is updated to this section identifier.
6. The content viewer scrolls to the corresponding section and updates the active section highlighting.
7. The contextual help/contact links are updated to include the new section value in their target URLs.

Workflow: Use help and contact links for refund questions
1. User is reading refunds.html and has a question about a specific part of the policy (for example, preorders).
2. User scrolls to or navigates to the relevant section using the table of contents.
3. The page state sets currentSection to the relevant section identifier and synchronizes it to the URL.
4. User clicks the "Need more help?" link.
5. The help-links component constructs a URL to help.html including origin=refunds_policy and section=currentSection as URL parameters.
6. The browser navigates to help.html with the parameters so that the help page can tailor its content.
7. Alternatively, the user clicks the "Contact support about a refund" link.
8. The help-links component constructs a URL to contact.html including origin=refunds_policy and section=currentSection (and optionally forwarding any existing URL parameters such as an orderNumber, if present).
9. The browser navigates to contact.html, where the contact form can pre-fill or pre-select a reason based on the passed parameters.

Workflow: Observe cart and wishlist status while reading policy
1. User opens refunds.html while having ebooks in their cart and wishlist.
2. On page load, the page calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary.
3. The header summary component displays the cart item count and total price from getCartSummary.
4. The component displays the wishlist item count from getWishlistSummary.
5. User can click the cart icon to navigate to the cart page or the wishlist icon to navigate to the wishlist page while keeping their place in policy history (using standard browser navigation to go back).
interactions:
Interaction: Page load initialization
- Trigger: Browser loads refunds.html.
- Behavior: The page-init component reads URL parameters (e.g., section and any other supported parameters), stores them in page state, calls getLegalPageContent with the correct pageType for the refunds and digital goods policy, and calls getCartSummary and getWishlistSummary. Once responses arrive, the policy content and header summaries are rendered. After content rendering, if a section parameter is present, the page scrolls to that section.

Interaction: Change policy section via URL (manual or external)
- Trigger: User opens refunds.html with a section query parameter or modifies the URL manually (e.g., using browser address bar or a copied link).
- Behavior: The page-init or routing logic parses the new URL, updates currentSection state, and notifies the content viewer and TOC components to set the active section and scroll accordingly. Contextual links update to include the new section in their targets.

Interaction: Click on a table-of-contents entry
- Trigger: User clicks a TOC entry representing a policy section.
- Behavior: The TOC component determines the section identifier associated with the clicked heading, updates the URL query parameter section to that identifier (using history APIs where available, without reloading the page), updates currentSection state, and requests the content viewer to scroll to the relevant section heading.

Interaction: Click "Need more help?" link
- Trigger: User clicks the "Need more help?" link at the bottom or within the policy page.
- Behavior: The help-links component reads the currentSection state and the current URL parameters, constructs a target URL for help.html that includes origin=refunds_policy and section=currentSection in the query string, and navigates the browser to that URL.

Interaction: Click "Contact support about a refund" link
- Trigger: User clicks the "Contact support about a refund" link.
- Behavior: The help-links component reads the currentSection state and forwards any relevant existing URL parameters (such as an orderNumber parameter if present in the current URL) when building a link to contact.html. It sets origin=refunds_policy and section=currentSection as query parameters and navigates to the resulting URL.

Interaction: Click cart icon in header
- Trigger: User clicks the cart icon in the header summary component.
- Behavior: The component navigates to cart.html (optionally including returnUrl=encoded current URL as a query parameter, so the user can be returned to refunds.html if needed). The cart content is not modified on this page; it only displays the summary from getCartSummary.

Interaction: Click wishlist icon in header
- Trigger: User clicks the wishlist icon in the header summary component.
- Behavior: The component navigates to wishlist.html (optionally including returnUrl=encoded current URL as a query parameter). The wishlist is not modified on this page; it only displays the summary from getWishlistSummary.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters to control state and deep-linking.

Supported URL parameters (design-level):
- section: Identifies which subsection of the Refunds & Digital Goods Policy should be brought into focus. Values are mapped to headings parsed from the policy body (e.g., a heading about preorder refunds might map to a section identifier such as 'preorders'). If section is missing or does not match any parsed section, the page defaults to showing the top of the policy without any specific section highlighted.
- origin, orderNumber, and other parameters: If present in the URL when the page loads, they are not modified by this page but may be forwarded to outgoing links (such as contact.html) where relevant, so downstream pages can maintain context. The refunds page itself does not interpret these parameters beyond forwarding them.

State derivation and updates:
1. On initial load, the page parses the current location.search string and extracts section and any other relevant parameters.
2. The parsed section value is stored in a page-level state variable currentSection.
3. After getLegalPageContent returns and the policy body is rendered, the content viewer component parses the body into a structured set of sections, each with a stable identifier derived from the headings (for example, by transforming a heading text into a URL-friendly slug). It builds a mapping from section identifiers to DOM anchors.
4. If currentSection matches one of the parsed section identifiers, the content viewer scrolls to that section and marks it as active; otherwise, it leaves the scroll at the top.
5. The table-of-contents component uses the same section mapping to display a clickable list of sections and to highlight the one matching currentSection.
6. When a user clicks a TOC entry, the TOC component updates currentSection and synchronizes it back to the URL's section parameter by rebuilding the query string and pushing or replacing the browser history state. The content viewer responds to currentSection changes by scrolling to the correct section.
7. The help-links component reads currentSection whenever it generates URLs for help.html and contact.html, adding section=currentSection to the outgoing URLs so the user's context is preserved.

The page does not use localStorage or any other client-side storage; the only persisted state used for navigation is contained in the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Refunds & Digital Goods Policy page by reading URL parameters, loading legal content for this policy, and loading cart and wishlist summaries. It then establishes the mapping between URL-driven section state and the rendered policy sections.

Displays a compact summary of the user's current cart and wishlist in the page header, using the data from the cart and wishlist summary interfaces. Provides navigation to the cart and wishlist pages.

Loads and renders the Refunds & Digital Goods Policy text, including the policy title and last-updated date, using the legal content interface. After rendering, it parses headings into logical sections and exposes a structured representation of these sections to other components.
Shows the policy's last-updated timestamp as provided by the legal content interface so users know how current the Refunds & Digital Goods Policy is.

Displays an automatically generated table of contents for the Refunds & Digital Goods Policy based on headings parsed from the loaded policy body. Allows users to jump to specific sections and keeps the active section in sync with URL parameters.

Synchronizes the current policy section view with the URL parameter and scroll position. When currentSection changes (from URL changes or TOC clicks), it scrolls to the corresponding anchor in the policy content. When the user scrolls manually, it can update currentSection based on the section in view, and update the URL accordingly.

Provides contextual navigation options for users who need additional help or want to contact support about a refund. It builds URLs to help.html and contact.html that include origin and section parameters, using the currentSection and any relevant existing URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px) centered within the viewport. The hero image spans full width; internal sections appear to align to a column grid that supports two-column product layouts.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the stacked, full-width flow from the mockup: a slim announcement bar and dark global header at the very top (existing site chrome), followed by a full-bleed hero band with the atmospheric bookstore photograph acting as the policy page masthead. Beneath the hero, the refunds policy content sits in a centered max-width container (≈1100–1200px) using a two-column layout: a narrower left column for navigation and support (policy section TOC and help links) and a wider right column for the main legal content and last-updated metadata. This mirrors the mockup’s left focal block + right content block pattern, replacing the featured book card with a prominent, utility-focused sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the stacked, full-width flow from the mockup: a slim announcement bar and dark global header at the very top (existing site chrome), followed by a full-bleed hero band with the atmospheric bookstore photograph acting as the policy page masthead. Beneath the hero, the refunds policy content sits in a centered max-width container (≈1100–1200px) using a two-column layout: a narrower left column for navigation and support (policy section TOC and help links) and a wider right column for the main legal content and last-updated metadata. This mirrors the mockup’s left focal block + right content block pattern, replacing the featured book card with a prominent, utility-focused sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 10–15% of the viewport height is used by the announcement bar and nav header, maintaining the dark, minimal chrome. The hero masthead with the bookstore image occupies about 40–50% of the initial viewport height—slightly more compact than a home hero but still immersive. A 48–64px gap separates the hero from the main content container. Inside the container, the two-column layout assigns about 35–40% of the width to the left column (TOC and help) and 60–65% to the right column (policy content). Vertical spacing between major content sections within the policy body follows the 32–48px rhythm, with 16–24px between related items to preserve a spacious but readable document feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header remains clean and airy, with comfortable padding around icons and cart/wishlist summaries. The hero has no heavy UI overlaid other than the page title and a short descriptor, so the photograph sets mood without clutter. In the main content area, density is moderate: the right column text is set within a single, readable column width, with generous line spacing and clear section breaks; the left column is concise, showing only key anchors and succinct help links. Overall, the page feels like a carefully typeset, editorial policy document rather than a dense legal wall of text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first encounters the bold announcement bar and then drops to the center logo in the dark header. From there, attention shifts right to the cart/wishlist summary area. As the user scrolls, the bookstore hero image and the centered policy title become the next focal point. Below the hero, the bright accent color and structural weight of the left column (TOC panel with a red-tinted header or accent stripe) catch the eye first, then the gaze moves horizontally to the right where the main policy title, timestamp, and legal copy live. As the user scrolls the document, the sticky left TOC remains in peripheral vision, with scroll-sync highlighting supporting a repeated left-to-right Z-flow between the active section label and the corresponding text on the right.

layout.overall_description: The Refunds & Digital Goods Policy page sits within the same dark-framed, image-forward shell as the storefront: a slim black announcement bar and charcoal navigation header span the top edge of the viewport, with the cart and wishlist summary controls anchored on the right. Immediately below, a full-bleed photograph of the bookstore fills nearly half the initial screen, softly darkened so that a centered, high-contrast page title and brief description can sit atop it. As you scroll past this masthead, the layout tightens into a centered, max-width content band. This band is split into two columns: on the left, a vertically oriented navigation panel with a red accent header and a clean list of policy section links, followed by compact help and support links; on the right, a spacious white content area presenting the policy text, with the “last updated” timestamp tucked near the top. The left panel remains visually grounded and mostly in view as you read, while the right column scrolls through clearly separated sections. The overall effect is a calm, editorial document experience framed by the atmospheric bookstore imagery and anchored by familiar e-commerce header utilities.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural wrapper for the page, establishing the full-bleed hero and the main content container. It positions the hero masthead immediately beneath the global announcement bar and navigation header, spanning 100% of the viewport width and roughly 40–50% of the viewport height. The hero uses the warm bookstore photograph as a background, slightly darkened, with any page-level title and intro text centered horizontally and vertically within this band, constrained to a roughly 60% width text block for readability. Below the hero, refunds-page-init introduces a vertical gap of about 48–64px, then places a max-width (≈1100–1200px) content container centered in the viewport. Within this container, it defines a two-column grid: a left column occupying about 35–40% of the width and a right column taking the remaining 60–65%, with a 32px gutter between them and 32–48px padding top/bottom inside the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural container that pins the hero at the top of the scroll and defines the responsive 12-column–like grid for the two-column layout; it adapts responsively so that below tablet widths the two columns stack vertically (left column content above right column content) with 32px vertical separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located within the existing dark navigation bar at the very top of the page, this component sits in the rightmost third of the header, aligned with the global content band. Horizontally, it occupies an area approximately 25–30% of the header’s inner width, starting from the right edge of the centered logo area and extending to the far right padding. Vertically, it is centered within the header’s 48–64px height, with 12–16px padding above and below. Cart and wishlist icons (and any numeric badges or dropdown summaries) are arranged left-to-right with 16–24px spacing between them, maintaining ample breathing room so they don’t visually compete with the logo. When expanded (e.g., on click), the summary panel drops down from this area, aligned to the right edge of the content band, with a white or near-white background and 16–24px internal padding, floating above the hero image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains fixed within the global header, which is typically sticky at the top of the viewport; any dropdown summary panels overlay content beneath the header, staying within the same max-width alignment as the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies the main right-hand column of the content container established below the hero. It begins just below the top padding of the container, aligned to the right of the 32px gutter from the left column, and spans roughly 60–65% of the container width. The content loader presents the policy title (if not already shown in the hero), introductory paragraph, and all subsequent policy sections in a single vertical flow. Each major section is separated by 32–48px of vertical space, with headings clearly distinguished and left-aligned. The text block maintains moderate line length (roughly 60–70 characters per line) and 16–24px line spacing to reinforce the editorial feel. Top padding of 16–24px above the first heading ensures a pleasant separation from the hero’s bottom edge, while bottom padding of 48–64px before the page footer preserves the airy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally within the page; its vertical position is used by the section-scroll-sync logic to map scroll positions to the corresponding entries in the left-hand TOC. On smaller screens where columns stack, policy-content-loader appears below the TOC and help links, spanning nearly 100% of the viewport width with consistent internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated indicator is placed within the right content column, near the top of the policy-content-loader area. It sits just below or to the right of the main policy title: typically aligned to the right edge of the text column, appearing as a small, subtle line of text (e.g., ‘Last updated: [date]’) in a muted grey. There is 8–12px of vertical spacing between the title and this indicator, and another 16–24px gap before the first body paragraph or section heading, so it reads as metadata without crowding the main content. Horizontally, it never exceeds about 30–35% of the column width, keeping it compact and understated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the policy-content-loader, moving with the content as the user scrolls; it has no sticky or floating behavior and simply acts as a reference anchor early in the document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies the upper portion of the left column beneath the hero, helping to visually echo the bold left-hand red block from the mockup. It is presented as a vertical panel starting near the top of the content container, aligned flush left with the container’s inner edge and spanning about 35–40% of the container width. At the top of the panel, a horizontal band 48–64px tall uses the primary red accent as a background, containing a concise label such as ‘On this page’ in white, left-aligned with 16–24px padding. Below this header, the TOC lists policy section titles as vertically stacked links on a white or very light background, each with 12–16px vertical padding and 8–16px between items. The panel has 16–24px inner padding on all sides, with a light border or subtle shadow to separate it from the page background. The bottom of the TOC panel transitions directly into the refunds-help-links block or leaves 16–24px of separation if visually distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the TOC panel becomes sticky after the user scrolls past the hero, maintaining a fixed position relative to the viewport’s top (with a 80–96px offset to clear the header) so that section links remain visible as the right column scrolls. On smaller screens where columns stack, the TOC appears above the main content and scrolls normally, dropping the sticky behavior to avoid reducing vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component provides behavioral glue between the right-column policy content and the left-column TOC, without introducing its own visible block. It conceptually overlays the main content area, tracking the scroll position of each policy section within the policy-content-loader and mapping it to the corresponding item in the policy-section-toc. Visually, its effect is seen as dynamic highlighting: the TOC entry matching the section currently near the top of the right content column is emphasized (e.g., bolded text, red left border, or red text color), while others remain in a neutral state. Clicking a TOC item triggers a smooth scroll in the right column to bring the corresponding section heading into view, aligned slightly below the global header to avoid being hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, logic-only layer that reacts to scrolling and clicks. It adjusts TOC highlighting and controls smooth scrolling, respecting the sticky offset of the left column and any fixed header height so that scroll targets land in a visually comfortable zone just below the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly beneath the policy-section-toc within the left column, this component serves as a compact contextual help block. It spans the same column width as the TOC and aligns perfectly with its left and right edges. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the TOC list if not visually merged. The block uses a white or very light background with a subtle border or top accent line in the primary red to tie it into the overall color system. Inside, a brief heading like ‘Need more help?’ is placed at the top with 8–12px of spacing below, followed by one to three concise links (e.g., contact support, view FAQs) stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. The entire block has 16–24px internal padding and ends with 24–32px of space before the bottom of the content container, giving the left column a well-balanced vertical composition relative to the taller policy text on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On large screens, refunds-help-links is part of the same sticky left column container as the TOC, so both the TOC and help links remain visible (or mostly visible) as the user scrolls through the policy text. On smaller screens where content stacks, it appears between the TOC and the main policy content, scrolling normally with the page.
